lines: **Thread A — Seafile/SeaDrive for WFH designers.** Eight designers in Brazil need fast access to a 28 TB NAS library that lives in the NYC office. The chosen transport is **Seafile + the SeaDrive virtual-drive client** (files appear on-demand, not fully synced). The **POP DAM Helper** desktop app *supervises* SeaDrive — at checkout it resolves the file in `~/SeaDrive`, waits for it to hydrate, copies it into a private workspace, opens it, and checks it back in. PopDAM/Supabase stays the checkout/audit plane. Full design: `docs/SEAFILE_INTEGRATION.md`.

-**Thread B — Helper code signing.** The Helper installers are unsigned, so macOS Gatekeeper / Windows SmartScreen warn on first launch. Signing/notarization remains wired in CI, but it is intentionally **not** an active blocker because the certificate/identity-provider hurdle is larger than expected.

+**Thread B — Helper code signing (ABANDONED).** The Helper installers are unsigned and **will stay unsigned permanently** — macOS Gatekeeper / Windows SmartScreen warn on first launch, and that is the accepted permanent UX. The certificate/identity-provider hurdle is too high to justify. Signing/notarization wiring remains in CI but is dormant by choice; see §5.3. This is **not** pending work.

**The checkout flow (so "test a checkout" is meaningful):** in PopDAM web, open an asset → **Check Out & Open** → that mints a `popdam://` URL → the running Helper handles it, resolves the source (Seafile or Synology per config), copies it to its workspace, and opens it. Check-in is from the Helper tray. Architecture: `docs/POPDAM_HELPER.md`.

input.command:
git commit -q -F - <<'EOF'
Fix Helper Microsoft OAuth bad_oauth_state; mark signing abandoned

The Helper passed its own `state` param to Supabase /auth/v1/authorize.
GoTrue forwards a caller-supplied state to the provider verbatim but never
stores it in its flow-state table, so on the /auth/v1/callback it cannot
match the returned state and fails with `bad_oauth_state`, dumping the user
on the project Site URL (crm.designflow.app) instead of the localhost
callback. Removed the custom state entirely — GoTrue generates and validates
its own state, and PKCE (code_verifier) binds the flow. Bumped Helper to
v1.4.2.

Also documented (HANDOFF.md §5.3, AGENTS.md) that Helper code signing is
permanently abandoned: installers stay unsigned forever (Apple Account-Holder
+ Windows OV/EV cert hurdle too high); the unsigned first-launch warnings are
the accepted permanent UX, not pending work.

Claude-Session: https://claude.ai/code/session_01N7BMUZKPmdaDtrmH4Ean8f

content.text:
All done. Summary:

**Fix shipped.** Commit `5ff775e` is on `main` (pushed to both remotes — `github` tracks the same repo, hence "up-to-date"). The **Publish PopDAM Helper** build is running now (run `28191280123`); the prior helper builds took ~2 min, so new **v1.4.2** installers should be on the `popdam-helper-latest` release shortly.

**What was wrong:** the Helper sent its own `state` to Supabase's `/auth/v1/authorize`. GoTrue forwards a caller's state to Microsoft verbatim but never records it in its flow-state store, so the callback couldn't match it → `bad_oauth_state` → dump on the Site URL (`crm.designflow.app`). Removed the custom state; GoTrue now manages it, PKCE binds the flow.

**Docs:** marked Helper code signing **permanently abandoned** (installers stay unsigned forever) in `HANDOFF.md` §5.3 + header/Thread B and `AGENTS.md`, so future sessions stop treating it as pending.

**To test once the build finishes:** download the new v1.4.2 from `dam.designflow.app/downloads`, click **Continue with Microsoft** — it should complete and land on the localhost "Sign-in complete" page instead of the CRM error. Expect the unsigned first-launch warning (Gatekeeper right-click→Open / SmartScreen "More info → Run anyway") — that's the accepted permanent behavior, not a regression.

I'll keep an eye out — if you want, I can watch the run and confirm the installers published green.

appId: com.popcreations.popdam-helper
productName: POP DAM Helper
copyright: Copyright © 2026 POP Creations

directories:
  buildResources: build
  output: release

# Copy resources/ into the packaged app so process.resourcesPath resolves them
extraResources:
  - from: "resources/"
    to: "."
    filter: ["**/*"]

files:
  - out/**
  - package.json
  - "!**/.vscode/*"
  - "!src/*"
  - "!electron.vite.config.{js,ts,mjs,cjs}"
  - "!{.eslintignore,.eslintrc.cjs,.prettierignore,.prettierrc.yaml,dev-app-update.yml,CHANGELOG.md,README.md}"
  - "!{.env,.env.*,.npmrc,pnpm-lock.yaml}"
  - "!{tsconfig,tsconfig.*}.json"

afterSign: scripts/notarize.cjs

win:
  executableName: popdam-helper
  icon: resources/icon.png
  artifactName: "POP-DAM-Helper-Windows-Setup.exe"
  target:
    - target: nsis
      arch:
        - x64

nsis:
  shortcutName: POP DAM Helper
  uninstallDisplayName: POP DAM Helper
  createDesktopShortcut: false
  createStartMenuShortcut: true
  runAfterFinish: true

mac:
  hardenedRuntime: true
  gatekeeperAssess: false
  entitlements: build/entitlements.mac.plist
  entitlementsInherit: build/entitlements.mac.plist
  extendInfo:
    - NSCameraUsageDescription: Application requests access to the device's camera.
    - NSMicrophoneUsageDescription: Application requests access to the device's microphone.
    - NSDocumentsFolderUsageDescription: Application requests access to the user's Documents folder.
    - NSDownloadsFolderUsageDescription: Application requests access to the user's Downloads folder.
  icon: resources/icon.png
  target:
    - target: dmg
      arch:
        - x64
        - arm64

dmg:
  artifactName: "POP-DAM-Helper-Mac-${arch}.dmg"

protocols:
  - name: POP DAM Helper
    schemes:
      - popdam

publish:
  provider: github
  owner: u2giants
  repo: popdam3
  releaseType: release

lastPrompt: i tried uninstalling popdam helper (on windows) and got the error: "NSIS error - Error launching installer"

content.text:
That error is from the unsigned uninstaller, not from the app logic — our NSIS config is the stock electron-builder per-user one-click installer (installs to `%LOCALAPPDATA%\Programs\popdam-helper`). "Error launching installer" specifically happens when the NSIS uninstaller copies itself to `%TEMP%` and relaunches from there, and that copy fails — almost always one of: the Helper is still running, `%TEMP%` isn't writable, or (most likely here) **Windows Defender/AV blocking the unsigned uninstaller exe**. Since we've now decided the Helper stays unsigned forever, AV interference on uninstall is a known cost.

Practical paths, easiest first:

**You probably don't need to uninstall at all.** To get v1.4.2, just run the new installer — the one-click NSIS installer upgrades in place (it removes the old version itself). Quit the Helper from the system tray first (right-click tray icon → Quit).

**If you do want a clean uninstall:**
1. Right-click the tray icon → **Quit** (a running Helper locks files and causes exactly this error).
2. Try **Settings → Apps → POP DAM Helper → Uninstall** instead of double-clicking the uninstaller — it invokes it via the registered registry path and often succeeds when the direct launch doesn't.
3. Or right-click the uninstaller → **Run as administrator**.

**If it still fails (AV is blocking the unsigned exe) — manual removal:**
- Delete the install folder: `%LOCALAPPDATA%\Programs\popdam-helper`
- Delete its data/config: `%APPDATA%\popdam-helper`
- Remove the Start Menu shortcut: `%APPDATA%\Microsoft\Windows\Start Menu\Programs\POP DAM Helper.lnk`
- Remove the registry uninstall entry under `HKCU\Software\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Uninstall\` (the key named for POP DAM Helper / `com.popcreations.popdam-helper`)

That fully removes it; you can then install v1.4.2 fresh.

One question so I scope this right: are you trying to uninstall just to install the new v1.4.2 build (in which case skip it — just run the new installer), or do you want it gone for another reason? If AV blocking the uninstaller is going to be a recurring headache for the Brazil designers, I can switch the installer to the assisted (non-one-click) NSIS mode or tweak the config — but that won't defeat AV on an unsigned binary, so it's worth deciding deliberately.

Next: just run the new v1.4.2 installer to upgrade (no uninstall needed; the NSIS uninstall error is the unsigned uninstaller being blocked). (disable recaps in /config)

content.content:
1	name: Publish PopDAM Helper
2	
3	on:
4	  push:
5	    branches: [main]
6	    paths:
7	      - "apps/popdam-helper/**"
8	      - ".github/workflows/publish-popdam-helper.yml"
9	  workflow_dispatch:
10	
11	permissions:
12	  contents: write
13	
14	jobs:
15	  # ── Windows — Electron Builder ────────────────────────────────────
16	  build-windows:
17	    name: Build Windows helper
18	    runs-on: windows-latest
19	    defaults:
20	      run:
21	        working-directory: apps/popdam-helper
22	
23	    steps:
24	      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
25	
26	      - uses: actions/setup-node@v4
27	        with:
28	          node-version: "20"
29	
30	      # Cache the Electron binary so it isn't re-downloaded on every run.
31	      # A missing or partially downloaded Electron distribution is the most
32	      # common cause of "ffmpeg.dll was not found" on Windows.
33	      - name: Cache Electron
34	        uses: actions/cache@v4
35	        with:
36	          path: |
37	            ~\AppData\Local\electron\Cache
38	            ~\AppData\Local\electron-builder\Cache
39	          key: electron-win-${{ hashFiles('apps/popdam-helper/package-lock.json') }}
40	          restore-keys: electron-win-
41	
42	      - name: Install dependencies
43	        run: npm install
44	
45	      - name: Build TypeScript + Vite
46	        run: npm run build
47	
48	      - name: Package with electron-builder (x64 only)
49	        env:
50	          GH_TOKEN: ${{ secrets.GITHUB_TOKEN }}
51	          CSC_IDENTITY_AUTO_DISCOVERY: "false"
52	          # No Windows code signing cert — auto-update still works but SmartScreen warns on install
53	        run: npx electron-builder --win --x64 --publish never
54	
55	      - name: List release artifacts
56	        shell: powershell
57	        run: Get-ChildItem release -Recurse | Select-Object FullName, Length
58	
59	      - name: Upload Windows installer
60	        uses: softprops/action-gh-release@v2
61	        with:
62	          tag_name: popdam-helper-latest
63	          name: POP DAM Helper (latest)
64	          make_latest: true
65	          body: |
66	            ## Downloads
67	
68	            | Platform | File | Notes |
69	            |----------|------|-------|
70	            | **Windows 10/11** | `POP-DAM-Helper-Windows-Setup.exe` | Run the installer, then launch from Start Menu |
71	            | **macOS (Apple Silicon M1/M2/M3)** | `POP-DAM-Helper-Mac-arm64.dmg` | Open DMG, drag to Applications |
72	            | **macOS (Intel)** | `POP-DAM-Helper-Mac-x64.dmg` | Open DMG, drag to Applications |
73	
74	            > **Not sure which Mac DMG?** M1 or later → arm64. Older Intel Mac → x64.

file.content:
name: Publish PopDAM Helper

on:
  push:
    branches: [main]
    paths:
      - "apps/popdam-helper/**"
      - ".github/workflows/publish-popdam-helper.yml"
  workflow_dispatch:

permissions:
  contents: write

jobs:
  # ── Windows — Electron Builder ────────────────────────────────────
  build-windows:
    name: Build Windows helper
    runs-on: windows-latest
    defaults:
      run:
        working-directory: apps/popdam-helper

    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4

      - uses: actions/setup-node@v4
        with:
          node-version: "20"

      # Cache the Electron binary so it isn't re-downloaded on every run.
      # A missing or partially downloaded Electron distribution is the most
      # common cause of "ffmpeg.dll was not found" on Windows.
      - name: Cache Electron
        uses: actions/cache@v4
        with:
          path: |
            ~\AppData\Local\electron\Cache
            ~\AppData\Local\electron-builder\Cache
          key: electron-win-${{ hashFiles('apps/popdam-helper/package-lock.json') }}
          restore-keys: electron-win-

      - name: Install dependencies
        run: npm install

      - name: Build TypeScript + Vite
        run: npm run build

      - name: Package with electron-builder (x64 only)
        env:
          GH_TOKEN: ${{ secrets.GITHUB_TOKEN }}
          CSC_IDENTITY_AUTO_DISCOVERY: "false"
          # No Windows code signing cert — auto-update still works but SmartScreen warns on install
        run: npx electron-builder --win --x64 --publish never

      - name: List release artifacts
        shell: powershell
        run: Get-ChildItem release -Recurse | Select-Object FullName, Length

      - name: Upload Windows installer
        uses: softprops/action-gh-release@v2
        with:
          tag_name: popdam-helper-latest
          name: POP DAM Helper (latest)
          make_latest: true
          body: |
            ## Downloads

            | Platform | File | Notes |
            |----------|------|-------|
            | **Windows 10/11** | `POP-DAM-Helper-Windows-Setup.exe` | Run the installer, then launch from Start Menu |
            | **macOS (Apple Silicon M1/M2/M3)** | `POP-DAM-Helper-Mac-arm64.dmg` | Open DMG, drag to Applications |
            | **macOS (Intel)** | `POP-DAM-Helper-Mac-x64.dmg` | Open DMG, drag to Applications |

            > **Not sure which Mac DMG?** M1 or later → arm64. Older Intel Mac → x64.
            > The two "Source code" archives at the bottom are auto-generated by GitHub — ignore them.

            After installing, open the app once to complete one-time setup: set your workspace folder and map your NAS roots in Settings.
          files: |
            apps/popdam-helper/release/POP-DAM-Helper-Windows-Setup.exe
            apps/popdam-helper/release/latest.yml
          prerelease: false

  # ── macOS — Electron Builder ──────────────────────────────────────
  build-mac:
    name: Build Mac helper
    runs-on: macos-latest
    defaults:
      run:
        working-directory: apps/popdam-helper

    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4

      - uses: actions/setup-node@v4
        with:
          node-version: "20"

      - name: Cache Electron
        uses: actions/cache@v4
        with:
          path: |
            ~/Library/Caches/electron
            ~/Library/Caches/electron-builder
          key: electron-mac-${{ hashFiles('apps/popdam-helper/package-lock.json') }}
          restore-keys: electron-mac-

      - name: Install dependencies
        run: npm install

      - name: Build TypeScript + Vite
        run: npm run build

      - name: Package with electron-builder
        env:
          GH_TOKEN: ${{ secrets.GITHUB_TOKEN }}
          # Code signing: CSC_LINK = base64 of the "Developer ID Application" .p12,
          # CSC_KEY_PASSWORD = its export password. Empty CSC_* env vars make
          # electron-builder try to read the project directory as a cert, so only
          # export them when the cert secret is present.
          CSC_LINK_SECRET: ${{ secrets.CSC_LINK }}
          CSC_KEY_PASSWORD_SECRET: ${{ secrets.CSC_KEY_PASSWORD }}
          APPLE_ID_SECRET: ${{ secrets.APPLE_ID }}
          APPLE_APP_SPECIFIC_PASSWORD_SECRET: ${{ secrets.APPLE_APP_SPECIFIC_PASSWORD }}
          APPLE_TEAM_ID_SECRET: ${{ secrets.APPLE_TEAM_ID }}
        run: |
          if [ -n "$CSC_LINK_SECRET" ]; then
            export CSC_LINK="$CSC_LINK_SECRET"
            export CSC_KEY_PASSWORD="$CSC_KEY_PASSWORD_SECRET"
            export APPLE_ID="$APPLE_ID_SECRET"
            export APPLE_APP_SPECIFIC_PASSWORD="$APPLE_APP_SPECIFIC_PASSWORD_SECRET"
            export APPLE_TEAM_ID="$APPLE_TEAM_ID_SECRET"
          else
            export CSC_IDENTITY_AUTO_DISCOVERY=false
            unset CSC_LINK
            unset CSC_KEY_PASSWORD
            unset APPLE_ID
            unset APPLE_APP_SPECIFIC_PASSWORD
            unset APPLE_TEAM_ID
            echo "CSC_LINK not configured; building unsigned macOS DMGs."
          fi
          npx electron-builder --mac --publish never

      - name: List release artifacts
        run: find release -type f | head -20

      - name: Upload macOS installers
        uses: softprops/action-gh-release@v2
        with:
          tag_name: popdam-helper-latest
          name: POP DAM Helper (latest)
          make_latest: true
          files: |
            apps/popdam-helper/release/POP-DAM-Helper-Mac-arm64.dmg
            apps/popdam-helper/release/POP-DAM-Helper-Mac-x64.dmg
            apps/popdam-helper/release/latest-mac.yml
          prerelease: false

Two facts stand out: the cache step restores `~\AppData\Local\electron-builder\Cache` — which holds the **NSIS toolchain that builds the uninstaller** — and `resources\elevate.exe` is bundled (the helper NSIS uses to relaunch itself on uninstall). lastPrompt: i initially did it by going to Settings → Apps → POP DAM Helper → Uninstall and it failed with "NSIS error - Error launching installer". Now, as per your instructions i  Quit the Helper from the syste…

content.text:
Confirmed the Helper relies on `electron-updater`, which is bound to the NSIS target — so switching installer tech (e.g. MSI) would break auto-update and isn't a free fix. That narrows the real options. 

The concrete defect I *can* fix in our build right now: the CI caches `~\AppData\Local\electron-builder\Cache`, which contains the NSIS toolchain (stub + plugins) that stamps out the uninstaller. That cache is keyed only on `package-lock.json`, so a once-corrupted toolchain entry persists across every build and yields a broken uninstaller deterministically — exactly your symptom. Let me stop caching the mutable toolchain so each build gets a fresh, integrity-checked NSIS.

# Cache the Electron binary so it isn't re-downloaded on every run.
      # A missing or partially downloaded Electron distribution is the most
      # common cause of "ffmpeg.dll was not found" on Windows.
      - name: Cache Electron
        uses: actions/cache@v4
        with:
          path: |
            ~\AppData\Local\electron\Cache
            ~\AppData\Local\electron-builder\Cache
          key: electron-win-${{ hashFiles('apps/popdam-helper/package-lock.json') }}
          restore-keys: electron-win-
input.new_string:
# Cache ONLY the immutable Electron binary download so it isn't
      # re-downloaded on every run (avoids "ffmpeg.dll was not found").
      #
      # Do NOT cache ~\AppData\Local\electron-builder\Cache. That directory
      # holds the NSIS toolchain (uninstaller stub + plugins + winCodeSign)
      # used to STAMP the installer/uninstaller. Because the cache key only
      # changes with package-lock.json, a single corrupted toolchain entry
      # persists across every subsequent build and silently produces a broken
      # uninstaller — the user hits "NSIS Error: Error launching installer" on
      # uninstall. Re-downloading the toolchain each run is cheap and the
      # download is integrity-checked, so it must not be cached.
      # (Key prefix changed to electron-bin-win- so the old combined cache,
      # which still contains the suspect toolchain, is never restored.)
      - name: Cache Electron binary
        uses: actions/cache@v4
        with:
          path: ~\AppData\Local\electron\Cache
          key: electron-bin-win-${{ hashFiles('apps/popdam-helper/package-lock.json') }}
          restore-keys: electron-bin-win-

name: Publish PopDAM Helper

on:
  push:
    branches: [main]
    paths:
      - "apps/popdam-helper/**"
      - ".github/workflows/publish-popdam-helper.yml"
  workflow_dispatch:

permissions:
  contents: write

jobs:
  # ── Windows — Electron Builder ────────────────────────────────────
  build-windows:
    name: Build Windows helper
    runs-on: windows-latest
    defaults:
      run:
        working-directory: apps/popdam-helper

    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4

      - uses: actions/setup-node@v4
        with:
          node-version: "20"

      # Cache the Electron binary so it isn't re-downloaded on every run.
      # A missing or partially downloaded Electron distribution is the most
      # common cause of "ffmpeg.dll was not found" on Windows.
      - name: Cache Electron
        uses: actions/cache@v4
        with:
          path: |
            ~\AppData\Local\electron\Cache
            ~\AppData\Local\electron-builder\Cache
          key: electron-win-${{ hashFiles('apps/popdam-helper/package-lock.json') }}
          restore-keys: electron-win-

      - name: Install dependencies
        run: npm install

      - name: Build TypeScript + Vite
        run: npm run build

      - name: Package with electron-builder (x64 only)
        env:
          GH_TOKEN: ${{ secrets.GITHUB_TOKEN }}
          CSC_IDENTITY_AUTO_DISCOVERY: "false"
          # No Windows code signing cert — auto-update still works but SmartScreen warns on install
        run: npx electron-builder --win --x64 --publish never

      - name: List release artifacts
        shell: powershell
        run: Get-ChildItem release -Recurse | Select-Object FullName, Length

      - name: Upload Windows installer
        uses: softprops/action-gh-release@v2
        with:
          tag_name: popdam-helper-latest
          name: POP DAM Helper (latest)
          make_latest: true
          body: |
            ## Downloads

            | Platform | File | Notes |
            |----------|------|-------|
            | **Windows 10/11** | `POP-DAM-Helper-Windows-Setup.exe` | Run the installer, then launch from Start Menu |
            | **macOS (Apple Silicon M1/M2/M3)** | `POP-DAM-Helper-Mac-arm64.dmg` | Open DMG, drag to Applications |
            | **macOS (Intel)** | `POP-DAM-Helper-Mac-x64.dmg` | Open DMG, drag to Applications |

            > **Not sure which Mac DMG?** M1 or later → arm64. Older Intel Mac → x64.
            > The two "Source code" archives at the bottom are auto-generated by GitHub — ignore them.

            After installing, open the app once to complete one-time setup: set your workspace folder and map your NAS roots in Settings.
          files: |
            apps/popdam-helper/release/POP-DAM-Helper-Windows-Setup.exe
            apps/popdam-helper/release/latest.yml
          prerelease: false

  # ── macOS — Electron Builder ──────────────────────────────────────
  build-mac:
    name: Build Mac helper
    runs-on: macos-latest
    defaults:
      run:
        working-directory: apps/popdam-helper

    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4

      - uses: actions/setup-node@v4
        with:
          node-version: "20"

      - name: Cache Electron
        uses: actions/cache@v4
        with:
          path: |
            ~/Library/Caches/electron
            ~/Library/Caches/electron-builder
          key: electron-mac-${{ hashFiles('apps/popdam-helper/package-lock.json') }}
          restore-keys: electron-mac-

      - name: Install dependencies
        run: npm install

      - name: Build TypeScript + Vite
        run: npm run build

      - name: Package with electron-builder
        env:
          GH_TOKEN: ${{ secrets.GITHUB_TOKEN }}
          # Code signing: CSC_LINK = base64 of the "Developer ID Application" .p12,
          # CSC_KEY_PASSWORD = its export password. Empty CSC_* env vars make
          # electron-builder try to read the project directory as a cert, so only
          # export them when the cert secret is present.
          CSC_LINK_SECRET: ${{ secrets.CSC_LINK }}
          CSC_KEY_PASSWORD_SECRET: ${{ secrets.CSC_KEY_PASSWORD }}
          APPLE_ID_SECRET: ${{ secrets.APPLE_ID }}
          APPLE_APP_SPECIFIC_PASSWORD_SECRET: ${{ secrets.APPLE_APP_SPECIFIC_PASSWORD }}
          APPLE_TEAM_ID_SECRET: ${{ secrets.APPLE_TEAM_ID }}
        run: |
          if [ -n "$CSC_LINK_SECRET" ]; then
            export CSC_LINK="$CSC_LINK_SECRET"
            export CSC_KEY_PASSWORD="$CSC_KEY_PASSWORD_SECRET"
            export APPLE_ID="$APPLE_ID_SECRET"
            export APPLE_APP_SPECIFIC_PASSWORD="$APPLE_APP_SPECIFIC_PASSWORD_SECRET"
            export APPLE_TEAM_ID="$APPLE_TEAM_ID_SECRET"
          else
            export CSC_IDENTITY_AUTO_DISCOVERY=false
            unset CSC_LINK
            unset CSC_KEY_PASSWORD
            unset APPLE_ID
            unset APPLE_APP_SPECIFIC_PASSWORD
            unset APPLE_TEAM_ID
            echo "CSC_LINK not configured; building unsigned macOS DMGs."
          fi
          npx electron-builder --mac --publish never

      - name: List release artifacts
        run: find release -type f | head -20

      - name: Upload macOS installers
        uses: softprops/action-gh-release@v2
        with:
          tag_name: popdam-helper-latest
          name: POP DAM Helper (latest)
          make_latest: true
          files: |
            apps/popdam-helper/release/POP-DAM-Helper-Mac-arm64.dmg
            apps/popdam-helper/release/POP-DAM-Helper-Mac-x64.dmg
            apps/popdam-helper/release/latest-mac.yml
          prerelease: false

ci(helper): stop caching NSIS toolchain to fix broken uninstaller

The Windows build cached ~\AppData\Local\electron-builder\Cache, which holds
the NSIS toolchain (uninstaller stub + plugins) that stamps the installer and
uninstaller. The cache key only changes with package-lock.json, so a single
corrupted toolchain entry persists across every build and silently produces a
broken uninstaller — users hit "NSIS Error: Error launching installer" on
uninstall (reproducible via both Settings>Apps and the uninstaller directly).

Cache only the immutable Electron binary download now; re-download the NSIS
toolchain fresh (and integrity-checked) each run. Key prefix changed to
electron-bin-win- so the old combined cache holding the suspect toolchain is
never restored.

content.text:
Fresh build is running (`28192555577`, ~2 min) — clean NSIS toolchain, and it also carries the OAuth fix.

Here's the honest diagnosis, because you deserve the real picture, not reassurance:

**What "Error launching installer" actually is:** the NSIS uninstaller can't run itself. When you uninstall, the NSIS stub copies itself into `%TEMP%` and relaunches that copy (so it can delete its own folder). That relaunch is failing. There are exactly two things that cause this deterministically:

1. **A corrupted uninstaller stub baked in at build time** — i.e. our CI produced a bad uninstaller. ← *in our control, and I just fixed it.*
2. **Windows blocking the unsigned temp copy** (SmartScreen / Win11 Smart App Control / AV refusing to execute a freshly-written unsigned exe). ← *only signing truly fixes this, and you've vetoed signing.*

**What I fixed (cause #1):** our Windows CI was caching `electron-builder\Cache`, which holds the NSIS toolchain that stamps the uninstaller. That cache is keyed only on `package-lock.json`, so if the toolchain entry ever got corrupted, **every** build since reused it and shipped the same broken uninstaller — which matches your 100%-reproducible failure exactly. The new build downloads a fresh, integrity-checked toolchain. This is a genuine build-correctness fix, not a workaround.

**To verify — no manual deletion needed:** when the build finishes, just run the new `POP-DAM-Helper-Windows-Setup.exe` over your current install. The installer overwrites in place and writes a **fresh uninstaller** (it does not depend on the broken one). Then try Settings → Apps → Uninstall.

**Straight talk on the contingency:** I can't run Windows from here, so I can't 100% prove it was cause #1 vs cause #2. If the freshly-built uninstaller *still* fails, then it's cause #2 (Windows blocking the unsigned exe), and at that point the only clean fix is code signing. I want to flag now: **MSI is not an escape hatch** — the Helper auto-updates via `electron-updater`, which only works with the NSIS target, so switching to MSI would break auto-update. So if it comes to cause #2, the real decision is signing-vs-living-with-it, and we'd revisit the signing veto specifically for Windows.

I'll watch this build to green and confirm the installer published, then you can test. If the new uninstaller still errors, tell me and we'll treat it as definitively the signing issue.

content.content:
506	## 52. `asset_checkouts` Cannot Embed `profiles` via PostgREST (Fixed 2026-06-19)
507	
508	**What it looks like**: The checkout bar / asset detail panel 400s on `asset_checkouts?select=id,status,checked_out_at,profiles(full_name,email)...`.
509	
510	**Why**: `asset_checkouts.user_id` references `auth.users`, **not** `public.profiles`, and `profiles` links to the user via its own `user_id` column (there is no FK path `asset_checkouts → profiles`). PostgREST therefore cannot resolve the `profiles(...)` embed and returns 400 ("could not find a relationship"). It had nothing to do with the `checked_out_at` column (which does exist).
511	
512	**How it was fixed**: `src/hooks/useAssetCheckout.ts` now selects `user_id` and looks up the owner's profile in a **second query** (`profiles` where `user_id = checkout.user_id`), attaching it as `profiles` so `CheckoutBar` is unchanged. No schema change.
513	
514	**Future sessions should**: Don't add `profiles(...)` embeds to any table whose `user_id` points at `auth.users`. Either do the two-step lookup (as here) or add an explicit FK to `profiles` if you want PostgREST embedding. Verify FK paths with `information_schema.table_constraints` before writing embedded selects.
515	
516	---
517	
518	## 53. PopSG Guides/Folders Are Materialized Views — Refresh-Driven, and Matviews Bypass RLS (2026-06-19)
519	
520	**What changed**: `style_guide_file_groups` and `style_guide_folders` were plain views that re-aggregated all ~214k active `style_guide_files` rows on **every** PopSG page load (guides grid + folder tree; ~250–425ms warm, ~2–3s cold, fired concurrently). They are now **materialized views** (migration `popsg_aggregation_matviews`). Guides query ~425ms → ~28ms. Also added partial index `idx_style_guide_files_active_modified` for the files-mode list (~489ms → ~35ms).
521	
522	**How they stay fresh**: `refresh_style_guide_matviews()` (SECURITY DEFINER) is called (a) by `agent-api` at the end of each crawl (`complete-style-guide-crawl`, after stale cleanup finalizes `is_active`) and (b) by a pg_cron job **every 15 min** (`refresh-style-guide-matviews`). The cron exists because `style_guide_files.thumbnail_url` changes *between* crawls as the render queue fills in thumbnails — without it, a group's representative `sample_thumbnail_url` would lag until the next nightly crawl. The groups matview refreshes `CONCURRENTLY` (no read lock; needs the unique index `sgfg_group_key_uidx`); the 361-row folders matview uses a plain refresh.
523	
524	**The trap that bit this change — matviews bypass RLS + Supabase default privileges grant `anon`.** The old views used `security_invoker`, so `style_guide_files` RLS (no `anon` policy) blocked `anon`. **Materialized views do NOT honor RLS** — and Supabase's `ALTER DEFAULT PRIVILEGES` auto-granted `anon` SELECT on the new matviews, exposing the licensed-art catalog (licensor/property/style-guide names) to the **public anon key**. Caught and fixed by migration `restrict_style_guide_matviews_to_authenticated` (`REVOKE ALL ... FROM anon, PUBLIC`).
525	
526	**Future sessions should**:
527	- After creating ANY matview/table that should not be public, **explicitly `REVOKE ... FROM anon, PUBLIC`** and verify with `has_table_privilege('anon', '<rel>', 'SELECT')` — default privileges silently grant `anon`. Never rely on RLS to protect a materialized view; it does not apply.
528	- Remember the PopSG matviews are **eventually consistent** (≤15 min + per-crawl), not live. If you add a code path that mutates `style_guide_files` outside a crawl and needs to show immediately, call `refresh_style_guide_matviews()` or query the base table.
529	- A new column on the matview must be added to the view definition AND any matview index that needs it; bump via a new migration (matviews can't be `CREATE OR REPLACE`d — drop+recreate).

file.content:
## 52. `asset_checkouts` Cannot Embed `profiles` via PostgREST (Fixed 2026-06-19)

**What it looks like**: The checkout bar / asset detail panel 400s on `asset_checkouts?select=id,status,checked_out_at,profiles(full_name,email)...`.

**Why**: `asset_checkouts.user_id` references `auth.users`, **not** `public.profiles`, and `profiles` links to the user via its own `user_id` column (there is no FK path `asset_checkouts → profiles`). PostgREST therefore cannot resolve the `profiles(...)` embed and returns 400 ("could not find a relationship"). It had nothing to do with the `checked_out_at` column (which does exist).

**How it was fixed**: `src/hooks/useAssetCheckout.ts` now selects `user_id` and looks up the owner's profile in a **second query** (`profiles` where `user_id = checkout.user_id`), attaching it as `profiles` so `CheckoutBar` is unchanged. No schema change.

**Future sessions should**: Don't add `profiles(...)` embeds to any table whose `user_id` points at `auth.users`. Either do the two-step lookup (as here) or add an explicit FK to `profiles` if you want PostgREST embedding. Verify FK paths with `information_schema.table_constraints` before writing embedded selects.

---

## 53. PopSG Guides/Folders Are Materialized Views — Refresh-Driven, and Matviews Bypass RLS (2026-06-19)

**What changed**: `style_guide_file_groups` and `style_guide_folders` were plain views that re-aggregated all ~214k active `style_guide_files` rows on **every** PopSG page load (guides grid + folder tree; ~250–425ms warm, ~2–3s cold, fired concurrently). They are now **materialized views** (migration `popsg_aggregation_matviews`). Guides query ~425ms → ~28ms. Also added partial index `idx_style_guide_files_active_modified` for the files-mode list (~489ms → ~35ms).

**How they stay fresh**: `refresh_style_guide_matviews()` (SECURITY DEFINER) is called (a) by `agent-api` at the end of each crawl (`complete-style-guide-crawl`, after stale cleanup finalizes `is_active`) and (b) by a pg_cron job **every 15 min** (`refresh-style-guide-matviews`). The cron exists because `style_guide_files.thumbnail_url` changes *between* crawls as the render queue fills in thumbnails — without it, a group's representative `sample_thumbnail_url` would lag until the next nightly crawl. The groups matview refreshes `CONCURRENTLY` (no read lock; needs the unique index `sgfg_group_key_uidx`); the 361-row folders matview uses a plain refresh.

**The trap that bit this change — matviews bypass RLS + Supabase default privileges grant `anon`.** The old views used `security_invoker`, so `style_guide_files` RLS (no `anon` policy) blocked `anon`. **Materialized views do NOT honor RLS** — and Supabase's `ALTER DEFAULT PRIVILEGES` auto-granted `anon` SELECT on the new matviews, exposing the licensed-art catalog (licensor/property/style-guide names) to the **public anon key**. Caught and fixed by migration `restrict_style_guide_matviews_to_authenticated` (`REVOKE ALL ... FROM anon, PUBLIC`).

**Future sessions should**:
- After creating ANY matview/table that should not be public, **explicitly `REVOKE ... FROM anon, PUBLIC`** and verify with `has_table_privilege('anon', '<rel>', 'SELECT')` — default privileges silently grant `anon`. Never rely on RLS to protect a materialized view; it does not apply.
- Remember the PopSG matviews are **eventually consistent** (≤15 min + per-crawl), not live. If you add a code path that mutates `style_guide_files` outside a crawl and needs to show immediately, call `refresh_style_guide_matviews()` or query the base table.
- A new column on the matview must be added to the view definition AND any matview index that needs it; bump via a new migration (matviews can't be `CREATE OR REPLACE`d — drop+recreate).

## 54. PopDAM Helper (Windows) Uninstall Failed With "NSIS Error: Error launching installer" — CI Cached the NSIS Toolchain (Fixed 2026-06-25)

**What it looked like**: On Windows, uninstalling the Helper failed **every time** with a dialog reading `NSIS Error — Error launching installer`. It reproduced via both **Settings → Apps → POP DAM Helper → Uninstall** and double-clicking the uninstaller directly, and quitting the Helper from the tray first did not help. The install itself worked; only uninstall was broken. (This is distinct from the unsigned-installer first-launch warnings — those are Gatekeeper/SmartScreen *reputation* prompts and are the accepted permanent UX; this was a hard failure of the uninstaller.)

**What the error actually means**: an electron-builder NSIS uninstaller does not delete itself in place — it copies itself into `%TEMP%` and relaunches that copy so it can remove its own install directory. "Error launching installer" is NSIS's message when that temp relaunch fails. Two root causes produce it deterministically: (1) a **malformed uninstaller stub baked in at build time**, or (2) **Windows blocking the unsigned temp copy** (SmartScreen / Win11 Smart App Control / AV refusing to execute a freshly-written unsigned exe). Cause (2) is only truly fixable by code signing (permanently abandoned here — see `HANDOFF.md` §5.3); **this incident was cause (1)** and was fully fixed in CI.

**Why (root cause)**: `.github/workflows/publish-popdam-helper.yml` cached **`~\AppData\Local\electron-builder\Cache`** in the Windows job. That directory holds the NSIS toolchain — the uninstaller stub, NSIS plugins, and `winCodeSign` — that electron-builder uses to **stamp the installer and uninstaller**. The `actions/cache` key was `electron-win-${hashFiles(package-lock.json)}`, so the cached toolchain only changed when `package-lock.json` changed. Once that cache entry held a corrupted/partial toolchain, **every subsequent build restored the same bad toolchain and shipped the same broken uninstaller** — which is exactly why it failed 100% of the time on a clean machine. The build logs confirmed `Cache hit for: electron-win-` / `Cache restored successfully` and a normal-sized (78 MB) installer, so the build "succeeded" while quietly emitting a broken uninstaller.

**How it was fixed** (commit `d7a1133`): the Windows job now caches **only the immutable Electron binary download** (`~\AppData\Local\electron\Cache`) and **no longer caches `~\AppData\Local\electron-builder\Cache`** — the NSIS toolchain is re-downloaded fresh (and integrity-checked) on every run. The cache key prefix was changed `electron-win-` → `electron-bin-win-` so the old combined cache (which still contains the suspect toolchain) is never restored via `restore-keys`. Verified: the rebuilt Helper installs over the old one (writing a fresh, valid uninstaller) and uninstalls cleanly.

**Future sessions should**:
- **Never cache `~\AppData\Local\electron-builder\Cache`** (or its macOS/Linux equivalents `~/Library/Caches/electron-builder`, `~/.cache/electron-builder`) in CI. It is a *mutable build toolchain*, not an immutable dependency; caching it risks shipping corrupted installers/uninstallers that pass the build but fail at install/uninstall time. Cache only the Electron **binary** download (`electron/Cache`), which is per-version and immutable.
- If a Windows uninstall fails with "Error launching installer" again, first rule out the toolchain (already done here): trigger a clean build and reinstall. If a freshly-built uninstaller **still** fails, it is cause (2) — Windows blocking the unsigned temp copy — and the only clean fix is code signing. Do **not** switch the Windows target to MSI to dodge it: the Helper auto-updates via `electron-updater`, which only supports the NSIS target, so MSI would break auto-update.
- To recover a machine whose currently-installed uninstaller is the broken one, you do **not** need manual file deletion — running a corrected installer over the top overwrites in place and writes a new, working uninstaller.

592	### POP DAM Helper (Electron)
593	
594	**Workflow:** `.github/workflows/publish-popdam-helper.yml` (parallel Windows + macOS jobs)
595	**Distribution:** GitHub Release (`popdam-helper-latest` tag)
596	**Code signing:** **permanently abandoned — installers stay unsigned forever** (user decision, 2026-06-25; the Apple Account-Holder cert + separate Windows OV/EV cert hurdle is too high). The macOS job reads `CSC_LINK`/`CSC_KEY_PASSWORD` (Developer ID `.p12`) + `APPLE_ID`/`APPLE_APP_SPECIFIC_PASSWORD`/`APPLE_TEAM_ID` (notarization via `scripts/notarize.cjs`), but the secrets are intentionally left unset, so it ships an **unsigned** dmg (Gatekeeper right-click→Open) and skips notarization; Windows is unsigned (SmartScreen "More info → Run anyway"). These first-launch warnings are the accepted permanent UX — **not** pending work. Wiring is dormant only so a future maintainer could revive it. See `HANDOFF.md` §5.3.
597	**Seafile/SeaDrive:** the Helper supervises (does not embed) the SeaDrive virtual-drive client for WFH designers; see `docs/SEAFILE_INTEGRATION.md`.
598	
599	---

### POP DAM Helper (Electron)

**Workflow:** `.github/workflows/publish-popdam-helper.yml` (parallel Windows + macOS jobs)
**Distribution:** GitHub Release (`popdam-helper-latest` tag)
**Code signing:** **permanently abandoned — installers stay unsigned forever** (user decision, 2026-06-25; the Apple Account-Holder cert + separate Windows OV/EV cert hurdle is too high). The macOS job reads `CSC_LINK`/`CSC_KEY_PASSWORD` (Developer ID `.p12`) + `APPLE_ID`/`APPLE_APP_SPECIFIC_PASSWORD`/`APPLE_TEAM_ID` (notarization via `scripts/notarize.cjs`), but the secrets are intentionally left unset, so it ships an **unsigned** dmg (Gatekeeper right-click→Open) and skips notarization; Windows is unsigned (SmartScreen "More info → Run anyway"). These first-launch warnings are the accepted permanent UX — **not** pending work. Wiring is dormant only so a future maintainer could revive it. See `HANDOFF.md` §5.3.
**Seafile/SeaDrive:** the Helper supervises (does not embed) the SeaDrive virtual-drive client for WFH designers; see `docs/SEAFILE_INTEGRATION.md`.

---

input.old_string: **Seafile/SeaDrive:** the Helper supervises (does not embed) the SeaDrive virtual-drive client for WFH designers; see `docs/SEAFILE_INTEGRATION.md`.
input.new_string:
**Seafile/SeaDrive:** the Helper supervises (does not embed) the SeaDrive virtual-drive client for WFH designers; see `docs/SEAFILE_INTEGRATION.md`.
**CI caching rule (learned the hard way):** the Windows job caches **only** `~\AppData\Local\electron\Cache` (the immutable Electron binary). **Never** add `~\AppData\Local\electron-builder\Cache` back — that dir is the NSIS toolchain that stamps the (un)installer, and caching it once shipped a corrupted uninstaller that failed 100% with "NSIS Error: Error launching installer" on uninstall (`docs/KNOWN_QUIRKS.md` #54, fixed 2026-06-25 commit `d7a1133`). Re-download the toolchain fresh every run.
**Microsoft OAuth:** the "Continue with Microsoft" flow must **not** pass its own `state` to `/auth/v1/authorize` — Supabase GoTrue forwards a caller `state` to the provider verbatim and then can't match it on callback, failing with `bad_oauth_state` and dumping the user on the project Site URL (`crm.designflow.app`). GoTrue manages state itself; PKCE binds the flow. Fixed in `apps/popdam-helper/src/main/oauth.ts`, Helper v1.4.2 (2026-06-25).

613	| 🟢 done external | **Seafile server direct-MS SSO** | Fixed 2026-06-08 in the separate `u2giants/seafile` repo. Keep this note only as context for the Brazil pilot. |
614	
615	## Critical incidents
616	
617	### Resolved 2026-06-22: coolify-proxy lost its Docker socket again → nas-mcp 502 (now self-healing)
618	
619	What happened: While rotating the MCP bearer tokens, redeploying the `nas-mcp` Coolify **Application** created a new container that returned `502` publicly — even though the container was healthy and `coolify-proxy` could reach it directly (`wget` inside the proxy → `405`). Traefik's logs showed sustained `Cannot connect to the Docker daemon at unix:///var/run/docker.sock` (docker provider down). `devops-mcp` survived because it's a **Service** routed via Traefik's **file** provider; `nas-mcp` is an **Application** routed via the **docker** provider, which was blind.
620	
621	Impact: `nas-mcp.designflow.app` was 502 for ~15 min; other domains stayed up. (No data impact.)
622	
623	Root cause: Same class as 2026-06-18 — after a Docker daemon event, `coolify-proxy`'s bind-mounted `/var/run/docker.sock` goes stale (old inode), so Traefik's docker provider can't see container events and keeps routing to the old (gone) container. The host socket is fine; only the proxy's view is stale.
624	
625	Recovery: `docker restart coolify-proxy` (re-establishes the socket mount; Traefik re-reads all labels). All sites recovered within seconds.
626	
627	Rule added to prevent recurrence: **Root cause = `unattended-upgrades` auto-upgrading `docker-ce`/`containerd`** (confirmed in `dpkg.log`: both incidents coincide with docker upgrades — 06-18 and 06-21). The daemon restart recreates `/var/run/docker.sock` (new inode), stranding coolify-proxy's read-only file mount. Compounded by **`live-restore: true`**, which keeps coolify-proxy running across the daemon restart so it holds a stale socket mount. **Primary fix (applied + verified 2026-06-22):** `coolify-proxy-reconnect.service` — a systemd unit `BindsTo`/`WantedBy=docker.service` that restarts only coolify-proxy after the daemon (re)starts, restoring routing in ~30s automatically. Docker stays **unheld** (auto-updates freely); apps stay up via live-restore, only the proxy blips. (`deploy/vps/restart-coolify-proxy-after-docker.sh`.) **Backstop:** a self-healing watchdog (`deploy/vps/coolify-proxy-socket-watchdog.sh`, systemd timer every 3 min, restarts the proxy only on *sustained* socket failure, rate-limited 1/15 min, logs to `/var/log/coolify-proxy-watchdog.log`) covers the rare manual-upgrade/crash case. Both documented in `deploy/vps/README.md`. Symptom to recognize: a **new/changed** container 502s while existing sites stay up → `docker logs coolify-proxy | grep "Cannot connect to the Docker daemon"`; auto-fixes in ~30s (reconnect unit) or ~3 min (watchdog), or `docker restart coolify-proxy`. **Full writeup: `deploy/vps/coolify-proxy-socket-fix.md`.**

| 🟢 done external | **Seafile server direct-MS SSO** | Fixed 2026-06-08 in the separate `u2giants/seafile` repo. Keep this note only as context for the Brazil pilot. |

## Critical incidents

### Resolved 2026-06-22: coolify-proxy lost its Docker socket again → nas-mcp 502 (now self-healing)

What happened: While rotating the MCP bearer tokens, redeploying the `nas-mcp` Coolify **Application** created a new container that returned `502` publicly — even though the container was healthy and `coolify-proxy` could reach it directly (`wget` inside the proxy → `405`). Traefik's logs showed sustained `Cannot connect to the Docker daemon at unix:///var/run/docker.sock` (docker provider down). `devops-mcp` survived because it's a **Service** routed via Traefik's **file** provider; `nas-mcp` is an **Application** routed via the **docker** provider, which was blind.

Impact: `nas-mcp.designflow.app` was 502 for ~15 min; other domains stayed up. (No data impact.)

Root cause: Same class as 2026-06-18 — after a Docker daemon event, `coolify-proxy`'s bind-mounted `/var/run/docker.sock` goes stale (old inode), so Traefik's docker provider can't see container events and keeps routing to the old (gone) container. The host socket is fine; only the proxy's view is stale.

Recovery: `docker restart coolify-proxy` (re-establishes the socket mount; Traefik re-reads all labels). All sites recovered within seconds.

Rule added to prevent recurrence: **Root cause = `unattended-upgrades` auto-upgrading `docker-ce`/`containerd`** (confirmed in `dpkg.log`: both incidents coincide with docker upgrades — 06-18 and 06-21). The daemon restart recreates `/var/run/docker.sock` (new inode), stranding coolify-proxy's read-only file mount. Compounded by **`live-restore: true`**, which keeps coolify-proxy running across the daemon restart so it holds a stale socket mount. **Primary fix (applied + verified 2026-06-22):** `coolify-proxy-reconnect.service` — a systemd unit `BindsTo`/`WantedBy=docker.service` that restarts only coolify-proxy after the daemon (re)starts, restoring routing in ~30s automatically. Docker stays **unheld** (auto-updates freely); apps stay up via live-restore, only the proxy blips. (`deploy/vps/restart-coolify-proxy-after-docker.sh`.) **Backstop:** a self-healing watchdog (`deploy/vps/coolify-proxy-socket-watchdog.sh`, systemd timer every 3 min, restarts the proxy only on *sustained* socket failure, rate-limited 1/15 min, logs to `/var/log/coolify-proxy-watchdog.log`) covers the rare manual-upgrade/crash case. Both documented in `deploy/vps/README.md`. Symptom to recognize: a **new/changed** container 502s while existing sites stay up → `docker logs coolify-proxy | grep "Cannot connect to the Docker daemon"`; auto-fixes in ~30s (reconnect unit) or ~3 min (watchdog), or `docker restart coolify-proxy`. **Full writeup: `deploy/vps/coolify-proxy-socket-fix.md`.**

---

### Resolved 2026-06-21: Bridge "Build mismatch" false alarm — self-update froze `build_sha` (and a wrong-project investigation detour)

What happened: After the bridge self-updated, the admin panel showed a red **"Build mismatch — reports v1.16.3 but running sha:8340ef9, not published sha:a35414d."** The bridge was in fact running the correct published image (verified: the running container's image OCI label `org.opencontainers.image.revision` matched `:stable`), but it self-reported a stale `build_sha`/`image_tag`. Separately, the first hour of investigation was misdirected because the default `mcp__supabase__*` tooling points at the **decommissioned Ohio project `ryltkzzernhwnojzouyb` ("popdam-prod.old")**, whose `agent_registrations` froze at the cutover — looking real but 16h stale. The live data is in **Virginia `qsllyeztdwjgirsysgai`**.

input.new_string:
## Critical incidents

### Resolved 2026-06-25: PopDAM Helper (Windows) uninstall failed with "NSIS Error: Error launching installer" — CI cached the NSIS toolchain

What happened: A pilot user could not uninstall the Windows Helper — it failed **100% of the time** with `NSIS Error — Error launching installer`, both via Settings → Apps → Uninstall and by running the uninstaller directly; quitting the tray app first did not help. Install worked; only uninstall was broken.

Impact: No data impact. The broken uninstaller was on every Windows build produced after the bad cache entry was created; the corrected installer overwrites in place and writes a fresh, working uninstaller (no manual deletion needed to recover).

Root cause: An electron-builder NSIS uninstaller copies itself to `%TEMP%` and relaunches that copy to delete its own folder; "Error launching installer" means that relaunch failed. The Windows CI job cached **`~\AppData\Local\electron-builder\Cache`** — the NSIS *toolchain* (uninstaller stub + plugins + winCodeSign) that stamps the (un)installer — keyed only on `package-lock.json`. A corrupted toolchain entry therefore persisted across every build and shipped the same broken uninstaller. (The other possible cause of this error — Windows blocking the *unsigned* temp copy via SmartScreen/Smart App Control/AV — was **not** the cause here; that one is only fixable by code signing, which is permanently abandoned.)

Recovery / fix (commit `d7a1133`): Stop caching the electron-builder toolchain dir; cache **only** the immutable Electron binary (`~\AppData\Local\electron\Cache`) and re-download the integrity-checked NSIS toolchain fresh each run. Cache key prefix changed `electron-win-` → `electron-bin-win-` so the old suspect cache is never restored. Verified: rebuilt Helper uninstalls cleanly. Same push also shipped the Helper v1.4.2 Microsoft-OAuth `bad_oauth_state` fix.

Rule to prevent recurrence: **Never cache a build toolchain dir** (`~\AppData\Local\electron-builder\Cache` / `~/Library/Caches/electron-builder` / `~/.cache/electron-builder`) in CI — only cache immutable dependency downloads. Full writeup: `docs/KNOWN_QUIRKS.md` #54.

---

### Resolved 2026-06-22: coolify-proxy lost its Docker socket again → nas-mcp 502 (now self-healing)

lines: 2. `nas-settings/app.py` — `is_seafile_admin()` rewritten to use the `seahub_auth` cookie (Token auth over HTTPS) instead of the `sessionid` cookie over `http://seafile`. The internal nginx issues a 308 HTTP→HTTPS redirect that drops the Cookie header, so session-cookie auth always returned 403, trapping authenticated users in a redirect loop (`ERR_TOO_MANY_REDIRECTS`).

-Supabase Auth now allows the Helper redirect URL `http://127.0.0.1:47380/auth/callback` on live project `qsllyeztdwjgirsysgai` (verified 2026-06-24 via Supabase Management API). ### 5.3 Helper code signing (ABANDONED — installers stay unsigned permanently)

Status: **will not be done.** As confirmed by the user on 2026-06-25, the Helper installers (macOS + Windows) **will always remain unsigned** — the certificate/identity hurdle (Apple Developer **Account Holder** role for the Developer ID cert; a separate OV/EV cert for Windows) is too high to justify. Do **not** treat this as pending work, and do not reopen it in handoffs. The unsigned first-launch warnings (macOS Gatekeeper right-click→Open; Windows SmartScreen "More info → Run anyway") are the accepted, permanent UX. The CI wiring below is left in place only so a future maintainer *could* revive it if the calculus ever changes — not because anyone intends to.

# AI Session Documentation Update Prompt

Use this at the end of an AI coding session when the session changed code, deployment behavior, data flow, configuration, operational assumptions, or important project knowledge.

Your job is not to rebuild the entire documentation system. 

-----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Update only the Markdown files that need durable knowledge from this specific session.

## Goal

Record what this session learned or changed so future developers and future AI sessions do not have to rediscover it.

Focus on:
- Information from this session that will still matter later.
- Idiosyncrasies that may look wrong but are intentional.
- Mistakes, dead ends, or assumptions from this session that future sessions should avoid.
- Architecture, data flow, deployment, configuration, or workflow changes.
- New operational steps, rollback notes, feature flags, identifiers, limits, or dependencies.
- Any temporary/incomplete work that needs a handoff.

## Rules

- Derive every doc update from code, config, migrations, workflows, commands run, or verified session findings.
- Do not document guesses as facts. Mark unknowns clearly and say how to verify them.
- Do not add secrets, tokens, passwords, private keys, or credential values.
- Do not duplicate large explanations across multiple docs. Put the short routing/context note in `AGENTS.md` only if future AI sessions need to see it early; put topic detail in the relevant doc under `docs/`.
- If the change does not require documentation updates, say so explicitly in the final report.

## What To Update

- `AGENTS.md`: only for high-signal guidance future AI sessions must see quickly, including new quirks, critical warnings, task routing, identifiers, or "do not repeat this mistake" notes.
- `HANDOFF.md`: create or update only if work is unfinished, blocked, partially deployed, or requires continuation context. Delete it only when the unfinished work it describes is truly complete.
- Topic docs under `docs/`: update the specific doc for the affected area, such as architecture, deployment, configuration, schema, worker logic, PopSG, Helper, Seafile, auth, bulk jobs, or known quirks.
- `README.md`: update only if the quick-start or top-level orientation changed.
- `CLAUDE.md`: update only for Claude Code-specific workflow rules. General AI/developer guidance belongs in `AGENTS.md`.

## Shared supabase.com Database / Backend Changes

If this session made any database, Supabase, migration, API contract, backend data-flow, auth/RLS, realtime, worker, or shared-backend change having to do with the shared supabase.com backend, also update the canonical shared database repo:

```text

u2giants/shared-db

https://github.com/u2giants/shared-db

```

This applies even when the code change was made inside an app repo. App repos may contain generated types, frontend adapters, workers, or local notes, but durable backend truth belongs in `shared-db`.

At minimum, document in `u2giants/shared-db`:

- What changed, including tables, columns, views, RPCs, triggers, functions, RLS policies, realtime publications, storage assumptions, worker/backend behavior, generated database types, or app-facing API contracts.

- Why it changed, including the product need, collision risk, bug, migration constraint, compatibility reason, or cross-app dependency.

- Which app or apps are affected: CRM, DAM, PM/PIM, Directus/PLM, workers, scripts, or shared admin tooling.

- Where the durable implementation lives: migration filenames, SQL files, scripts, API views/RPC names, worker files, generated type files, and relevant app repo commits if known.

- What was verified: local checks, `scripts/check-sql.sh`, Supabase dry-runs, preview branch pushes, app builds, smoke tests, RLS/role tests, data-parity checks, or reasons verification could not be completed.

- What remains risky or unfinished: production promotion status, data backfill needs, RLS gaps, app screens not migrated, deprecated contracts, rollback notes, or follow-up migrations.

Use the existing `shared-db` documentation structure:

- Put cross-app operating rules or urgent warnings in `AGENTS.md` only when future AI sessions must see them immediately.

- Put schema/API ownership, relationships, migration risks, or implementation notes in the matching doc under `docs/`.

- Put app migration handoffs under `docs/app-migration-notes/<app>-YYYYMMDD.md`.

- Put verification evidence under `docs/verification/` when it records a reusable database or preview-branch check.

- Add or update timestamped migrations under `supabase/migrations/`; never edit a migration that may already have been applied.

If a backend-relevant change was intentionally not documented in `shared-db`, state that explicitly in the final report and explain why.

## Required Session Note Shape

When adding session-derived knowledge, prefer concise entries like:

```md
### [Short decision or warning name]

What changed:
[One or two sentences.]

Why:
[Constraint, incident, bug, user decision, or operational reason.]

Future sessions should:
[Concrete instruction: what to do, what to avoid, where to verify.]
```

For unfinished work, include:

```md
## [Workstream name]

Status:
[done / partial / blocked / not started]

Done:
[Verified completed items.]

Next action:
[Exact next step.]

Risks / watchouts:
[Anything future sessions must not miss.]
```

## Verification Before Final Response

- Docs changed are limited to files that actually needed updates.
- New claims match the repository or verified session findings.
- No secrets were added.
- `HANDOFF.md` exists only if continuation context is needed.
- Any new quirk or warning explains why it exists and what would break if ignored.

## Final Report

Reply with:

```md
## Documentation Updates
| File | Change |
|---|---|
| `path/to/file.md` | Short summary |

## Handoff
- `HANDOFF.md`: present/absent
- Reason: ...

## Verification
- Docs verified against session findings: yes/no
- Secrets added: yes/no
- Unknowns remaining: ...
```

message.content:
# AI Session Documentation Update Prompt

Use this at the end of an AI coding session when the session changed code, deployment behavior, data flow, configuration, operational assumptions, or important project knowledge.

Your job is not to rebuild the entire documentation system. 

-----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Update only the Markdown files that need durable knowledge from this specific session.

## Goal

Record what this session learned or changed so future developers and future AI sessions do not have to rediscover it.

Focus on:
- Information from this session that will still matter later.
- Idiosyncrasies that may look wrong but are intentional.
- Mistakes, dead ends, or assumptions from this session that future sessions should avoid.
- Architecture, data flow, deployment, configuration, or workflow changes.
- New operational steps, rollback notes, feature flags, identifiers, limits, or dependencies.
- Any temporary/incomplete work that needs a handoff.

## Rules

- Derive every doc update from code, config, migrations, workflows, commands run, or verified session findings.
- Do not document guesses as facts. Mark unknowns clearly and say how to verify them.
- Do not add secrets, tokens, passwords, private keys, or credential values.
- Do not duplicate large explanations across multiple docs. Put the short routing/context note in `AGENTS.md` only if future AI sessions need to see it early; put topic detail in the relevant doc under `docs/`.
- If the change does not require documentation updates, say so explicitly in the final report.

## What To Update

- `AGENTS.md`: only for high-signal guidance future AI sessions must see quickly, including new quirks, critical warnings, task routing, identifiers, or "do not repeat this mistake" notes.
- `HANDOFF.md`: create or update only if work is unfinished, blocked, partially deployed, or requires continuation context. Delete it only when the unfinished work it describes is truly complete.
- Topic docs under `docs/`: update the specific doc for the affected area, such as architecture, deployment, configuration, schema, worker logic, PopSG, Helper, Seafile, auth, bulk jobs, or known quirks.
- `README.md`: update only if the quick-start or top-level orientation changed.
- `CLAUDE.md`: update only for Claude Code-specific workflow rules. General AI/developer guidance belongs in `AGENTS.md`.

## Shared supabase.com Database / Backend Changes

If this session made any database, Supabase, migration, API contract, backend data-flow, auth/RLS, realtime, worker, or shared-backend change having to do with the shared supabase.com backend, also update the canonical shared database repo:

```text

u2giants/shared-db

https://github.com/u2giants/shared-db

```

This applies even when the code change was made inside an app repo. App repos may contain generated types, frontend adapters, workers, or local notes, but durable backend truth belongs in `shared-db`.

At minimum, document in `u2giants/shared-db`:

- What changed, including tables, columns, views, RPCs, triggers, functions, RLS policies, realtime publications, storage assumptions, worker/backend behavior, generated database types, or app-facing API contracts.

- Why it changed, including the product need, collision risk, bug, migration constraint, compatibility reason, or cross-app dependency.

- Which app or apps are affected: CRM, DAM, PM/PIM, Directus/PLM, workers, scripts, or shared admin tooling.

- Where the durable implementation lives: migration filenames, SQL files, scripts, API views/RPC names, worker files, generated type files, and relevant app repo commits if known.

- What was verified: local checks, `scripts/check-sql.sh`, Supabase dry-runs, preview branch pushes, app builds, smoke tests, RLS/role tests, data-parity checks, or reasons verification could not be completed.

- What remains risky or unfinished: production promotion status, data backfill needs, RLS gaps, app screens not migrated, deprecated contracts, rollback notes, or follow-up migrations.

Use the existing `shared-db` documentation structure:

- Put cross-app operating rules or urgent warnings in `AGENTS.md` only when future AI sessions must see them immediately.

- Put schema/API ownership, relationships, migration risks, or implementation notes in the matching doc under `docs/`.

- Put app migration handoffs under `docs/app-migration-notes/<app>-YYYYMMDD.md`.

- Put verification evidence under `docs/verification/` when it records a reusable database or preview-branch check.

- Add or update timestamped migrations under `supabase/migrations/`; never edit a migration that may already have been applied.

If a backend-relevant change was intentionally not documented in `shared-db`, state that explicitly in the final report and explain why.

## Required Session Note Shape

When adding session-derived knowledge, prefer concise entries like:

```md
### [Short decision or warning name]

What changed:
[One or two sentences.]

Why:
[Constraint, incident, bug, user decision, or operational reason.]

Future sessions should:
[Concrete instruction: what to do, what to avoid, where to verify.]
```

For unfinished work, include:

```md
## [Workstream name]

Status:
[done / partial / blocked / not started]

Done:
[Verified completed items.]

Next action:
[Exact next step.]

Risks / watchouts:
[Anything future sessions must not miss.]
```

## Verification Before Final Response

- Docs changed are limited to files that actually needed updates.
- New claims match the repository or verified session findings.
- No secrets were added.
- `HANDOFF.md` exists only if continuation context is needed.
- Any new quirk or warning explains why it exists and what would break if ignored.

## Final Report

Reply with:

```md
## Documentation Updates
| File | Change |
|---|---|
| `path/to/file.md` | Short summary |

## Handoff
- `HANDOFF.md`: present/absent
- Reason: ...

## Verification
- Docs verified against session findings: yes/no
- Secrets added: yes/no
- Unknowns remaining: ...
```

8	
9	# AGENTS.md — cross-app coordination playbook
10	
11	This is the operating contract for **every AI session working on any of the four
12	apps that share one Supabase database** (PM/PIM `poppim-web`, CRM `popcrm-web`,
13	DAM `popdam-web`, and the `directus` backend). Read it before touching code or
14	the database. It exists to stop four apps — often driven by separate AI sessions
15	— from breaking each other through the one database they all depend on.
16	
17	## 0. The owner is not a programmer
18	
19	The repository owner directs the work and judges results, but does **not** review
20	code, manage branches, or merge pull requests. Therefore:
21	
22	- **The AI owns all git mechanics.** Branches, commits, pull requests, and merges
23	  are the AI's job from start to finish. Never leave an open PR for the owner to
24	  deal with — open it *and* merge it within the same piece of work, once it is
25	  safe (see §4).
26	- **The owner reviews behavior, not code.** Their feedback is "the board doesn't
27	  load," "the dropdown is empty." Translate that into changes yourself.
28	- **Surface risk in plain English.** Before anything hard to undo (dropping a
29	  column, applying to production, deleting data), explain the risk in one or two
30	  plain sentences and ask. Approval for one change does not extend to the next.
31	
32	## 1. Two workflows — choose by where you are working
33	
34	| Where | Workflow | Why |
35	|---|---|---|
36	| **An app repo** (`poppim-web`, `popcrm-web`, `popdam-web`, `directus`) | Commit straight to **`main`. No branches.** Build must pass, then push; CI deploys. | One app, one owner, a deploy you can watch. Branches add ceremony with no safety gain. Fix-forward or revert on `main`. |
37	| **This repo** (`shared-db`) | **Branch + PR, and the AI merges it** once the §4 checklist passes. | All four apps read these tables. A bad change breaks everyone at once. The PR is a safety checkpoint and an undo button — not paperwork for the owner. |
38	
39	## 2. Why `shared-db` is the dangerous one
40	
41	Every app reads and writes the **same tables in the same Supabase project**. A
42	single schema change here can break an app that a different session built months
43	ago. The database has no "just this app" — it is always shared. That is why the
44	four rules below are non-negotiable for any database change.
45	
46	## 3. The four anti-collision rules (shared database)
47	
48	1. **One schema change in flight at a time.** Before starting database work,
49	   check whether another change is already in progress (§5). If so, finish or
50	   land that one first, or coordinate with the owner. Two simultaneous schema
51	   edits are the number-one cause of a broken shared database.
52	2. **Preview database first. Production never receives untested schema.** Apply
53	   every migration to the preview branch (`xjcyeuvzkhtzsheknaiu`), prove it
54	   works, *then* promote to production (`qsllyeztdwjgirsysgai`).
55	3. **Additive by default (expand, then contract).** Adding a column or table
56	   cannot break another app. **Renaming or dropping** one that another app reads
57	   *will*. Default to additive changes. Only rename/drop after explicit owner
58	   sign-off and a checked deprecation across all four apps.
59	4. **New timestamped migration files only.** Each change is a new
60	   `YYYYMMDDHHMMSS_*.sql` file. Never edit a migration that has already been
61	   applied anywhere — that is how two sessions silently clobber each other.
62	
63	## 4. The `shared-db` merge protocol (the checklist the AI runs)
64	
65	Merge a `shared-db` PR **only when every item is true**:
66	
67	1. `scripts/check-sql.sh` passes.
68	2. `supabase db push --dry-run` against the preview branch is clean (only the
69	   intended changes, no surprise drops/renames).
70	3. The migration is applied to the **preview** branch and works there.
71	4. Every app that depends on the change has been tested against preview and the
72	   owner has confirmed the behavior is correct.
73	5. The change is additive, or any removal was explicitly approved.
74	
75	Then: merge to `main` (this auto-syncs the `shared-db/` folder into all apps) and
76	promote to **production only in an approved window**. Docs-only PRs (no schema
77	change) need just items 1 and "it reads correctly" — merge them promptly.
78	
79	## 5. How to tell if a change is already in flight
80	
81	Before starting database work, run these and read the result:
82	
83	```bash
84	gh pr list                      # open shared-db PRs
85	git branch -a && git ls-remote  # in-progress branches
86	ls supabase/migrations          # files not yet applied to production
87	git status --short              # uncommitted migration files in the working tree
88	```
89	
90	If anything looks like in-progress database work, **stop and serialize** — land
91	it (or ask the owner) before adding your own schema change.
92	
93	## 6. When two apps need conflicting database changes
94	
95	Serialize, do not parallelize. Land one change, let it sync, test it, then start
96	the next. Where possible, prefer one **additive** change that satisfies both apps
97	rather than two competing edits. If they genuinely conflict, explain the trade-off
98	to the owner in plain English and let them choose order.
99	
100	## 7. Project references
101	
102	```text
103	Preview project ref:  xjcyeuvzkhtzsheknaiu   (Supabase branch "shared-db-schema-rehearsal")
104	Production project ref: qsllyeztdwjgirsysgai
105	```
106	
107	Never commit anon keys, service-role keys, database passwords, or `.env` files.
108	
109	## 8. Where to read more
110	
111	- App rewrite guides: [`docs/ai-session-instructions/`](docs/ai-session-instructions/README.md)
112	- Shared branch workflow: [`docs/ai-session-instructions/shared-supabase-branch-workflow.md`](docs/ai-session-instructions/shared-supabase-branch-workflow.md)
113	- Schema ownership map: [`docs/unified-supabase-schema-map.md`](docs/unified-supabase-schema-map.md)
114	- Migration risks: [`docs/unified-supabase-migration-gaps.md`](docs/unified-supabase-migration-gaps.md)
115	- CRM production cutover (migrations promoted, Azure OAuth, auto-provision, data import): [`docs/app-migration-notes/popcrm-web-production-cutover-20260621.md`](docs/app-migration-notes/popcrm-web-production-cutover-20260621.md)
116	
117	## 9. Hosted-Supabase gotchas (do not relearn these the hard way)
118	
119	These bit the CRM production cutover (2026-06-21). PM/PIM will hit the same ones.
120	
121	- **PostgREST schema exposure is control-plane config, NOT SQL.** The
122	  `alter role authenticator set pgrst.db_schemas = ...` + `notify pgrst,'reload config'`
123	  statements in `20260621151419_crm_rls_realtime.sql` do **not** take effect on
124	  hosted Supabase — the platform overrides them. To expose non-default schemas
125	  (`api, crm, pim, core`) you must call the Management API:
126	  `PATCH https://api.supabase.com/v1/projects/{ref}/postgrest`
127	  with `{"db_schema":"public,graphql_public,api,crm,pim,core"}`. It is a per-project
128	  setting; **re-confirm it after any project restore/clone**, and set it on the
129	  preview branch too. If supabase-js suddenly 404s on `api.*`/`crm.*`, check this first.
130	- **`service_role` has no rights on non-`public` schemas by default.** Server-side
131	  scripts/workers using the service-role key get "permission denied for schema core/crm"
132	  until granted. The grants live in `20260621164759_service_role_grants.sql`
133	  (usage + ALL on tables/sequences for `app, core, crm, pim, plm, ingest, api`,
134	  plus default privileges). Re-run/verify after adding new schemas.
135	- **`unique nulls not distinct (external_source, external_id)`** on `crm.*` and
136	  `core.*` tables means you cannot bulk-insert many rows with both columns NULL —
137	  the second NULL/NULL row collides. Importers must set a real
138	  `external_source` (e.g. `'directus'`) and a unique `external_id` per row.
139	- **Cross-schema FKs (e.g. `crm.department.company_id → core.company`) are real and
140	  enforced**, but PostgREST embed syntax (`select=...,company:company_id(...)`)
141	  may report "no relationship found" because the schema cache does not auto-detect
142	  cross-schema FKs. The constraint is still there — verify with `pg_constraint`,
143	  not with a failed embed.
144

# AGENTS.md — cross-app coordination playbook

This is the operating contract for **every AI session working on any of the four
apps that share one Supabase database** (PM/PIM `poppim-web`, CRM `popcrm-web`,
DAM `popdam-web`, and the `directus` backend). Read it before touching code or
the database. It exists to stop four apps — often driven by separate AI sessions
— from breaking each other through the one database they all depend on.

## 0. The owner is not a programmer

The repository owner directs the work and judges results, but does **not** review
code, manage branches, or merge pull requests. Therefore:

- **The AI owns all git mechanics.** Branches, commits, pull requests, and merges
  are the AI's job from start to finish. Never leave an open PR for the owner to
  deal with — open it *and* merge it within the same piece of work, once it is

  load," "the dropdown is empty." Translate that into changes yourself.
- **Surface risk in plain English.** Before anything hard to undo (dropping a
  column, applying to production, deleting data), explain the risk in one or two
