## 1. Two workflows — choose by where you are working

| Where | Workflow | Why |
|---|---|---|
| **An app repo** (`poppim-web`, `popcrm-web`, `popdam-web`, `directus`) | Commit straight to **`main`. No branches.** Build must pass, then push; CI deploys. | One app, one owner, a deploy you can watch. Branches add ceremony with no safety gain. Fix-forward or revert on `main`. |
| **This repo** (`shared-db`) | **Branch + PR, and the AI merges it** once the §4 checklist passes. | All four apps read these tables. A bad change breaks everyone at once. The PR is a safety checkpoint and an undo button — not paperwork for the owner. |

## 2. Why `shared-db` is the dangerous one

Every app reads and writes the **same tables in the same Supabase project**. A
single schema change here can break an app that a different session built months
ago. The database has no "just this app" — it is always shared. That is why the
four rules below are non-negotiable for any database change.

## 3. The four anti-collision rules (shared database)

1. **One schema change in flight at a time.** Before starting database work,
   check whether another change is already in progress (§5). If so, finish or
   land that one first, or coordinate with the owner. Two simultaneous schema
   edits are the number-one cause of a broken shared database.
2. **Preview database first. Production never receives untested schema.** Apply
   every migration to the preview branch (`xjcyeuvzkhtzsheknaiu`), prove it
   works, *then* promote to production (`qsllyeztdwjgirsysgai`).
3. **Additive by default (expand, then contract).** Adding a column or table
   cannot break another app. **Renaming or dropping** one that another app reads
   *will*. Default to additive changes. Only rename/drop after explicit owner
   sign-off and a checked deprecation across all four apps.
4. **New timestamped migration files only.** Each change is a new
   `YYYYMMDDHHMMSS_*.sql` file. Never edit a migration that has already been
   applied anywhere — that is how two sessions silently clobber each other.

## 4. The `shared-db` merge protocol (the checklist the AI runs)

Merge a `shared-db` PR **only when every item is true**:

1. `scripts/check-sql.sh` passes.
2. `supabase db push --dry-run` against the preview branch is clean (only the
   intended changes, no surprise drops/renames).
3. The migration is applied to the **preview** branch and works there.
4. Every app that depends on the change has been tested against preview and the
   owner has confirmed the behavior is correct.
5. The change is additive, or any removal was explicitly approved.

Then: merge to `main` (this auto-syncs the `shared-db/` folder into all apps) and
promote to **production only in an approved window**. Docs-only PRs (no schema
change) need just items 1 and "it reads correctly" — merge them promptly.

## 5. How to tell if a change is already in flight

Before starting database work, run these and read the result:

```bash
gh pr list                      # open shared-db PRs
git branch -a && git ls-remote  # in-progress branches
ls supabase/migrations          # files not yet applied to production
git status --short              # uncommitted migration files in the working tree
```

If anything looks like in-progress database work, **stop and serialize** — land
it (or ask the owner) before adding your own schema change.

## 6. When two apps need conflicting database changes

Serialize, do not parallelize. Land one change, let it sync, test it, then start
the next. Where possible, prefer one **additive** change that satisfies both apps
rather than two competing edits. If they genuinely conflict, explain the trade-off
to the owner in plain English and let them choose order.

## 7. Project references

```text
Preview project ref:  xjcyeuvzkhtzsheknaiu   (Supabase branch "shared-db-schema-rehearsal")
Production project ref: qsllyeztdwjgirsysgai
```

Never commit anon keys, service-role keys, database passwords, or `.env` files.

## 8. Where to read more

- App rewrite guides: [`docs/ai-session-instructions/`](docs/ai-session-instructions/README.md)
- Shared branch workflow: [`docs/ai-session-instructions/shared-supabase-branch-workflow.md`](docs/ai-session-instructions/shared-supabase-branch-workflow.md)
- Schema ownership map: [`docs/unified-supabase-schema-map.md`](docs/unified-supabase-schema-map.md)
- Migration risks: [`docs/unified-supabase-migration-gaps.md`](docs/unified-supabase-migration-gaps.md)
- CRM production cutover (migrations promoted, Azure OAuth, auto-provision, data import): [`docs/app-migration-notes/popcrm-web-production-cutover-20260621.md`](docs/app-migration-notes/popcrm-web-production-cutover-20260621.md)

## 9. Hosted-Supabase gotchas (do not relearn these the hard way)

These bit the CRM production cutover (2026-06-21). PM/PIM will hit the same ones.

- **PostgREST schema exposure is control-plane config, NOT SQL.** The
  `alter role authenticator set pgrst.db_schemas = ...` + `notify pgrst,'reload config'`
  statements in `20260621151419_crm_rls_realtime.sql` do **not** take effect on
  hosted Supabase — the platform overrides them. To expose non-default schemas
  (`api, crm, pim, core`) you must call the Management API:
  `PATCH https://api.supabase.com/v1/projects/{ref}/postgrest`
  with `{"db_schema":"public,graphql_public,api,crm,pim,core"}`. It is a per-project
  setting; **re-confirm it after any project restore/clone**, and set it on the
  preview branch too. If supabase-js suddenly 404s on `api.*`/`crm.*`, check this first.
- **`service_role` has no rights on non-`public` schemas by default.** Server-side
  scripts/workers using the service-role key get "permission denied for schema core/crm"
  until granted. The grants live in `20260621164759_service_role_grants.sql`
  (usage + ALL on tables/sequences for `app, core, crm, pim, plm, ingest, api`,
  plus default privileges). Re-run/verify after adding new schemas.
- **`unique nulls not distinct (external_source, external_id)`** on `crm.*` and
  `core.*` tables means you cannot bulk-insert many rows with both columns NULL —
  the second NULL/NULL row collides. Importers must set a real
  `external_source` (e.g. `'directus'`) and a unique `external_id` per row.
- **Cross-schema FKs (e.g. `crm.department.company_id → core.company`) are real and
  enforced**, but PostgREST embed syntax (`select=...,company:company_id(...)`)
  may report "no relationship found" because the schema cache does not auto-detect
  cross-schema FKs. The constraint is still there — verify with `pg_constraint`,
  not with a failed embed.

content.content:
146	Used to call the `helper-api` edge function (checkout tracking, heartbeat, device registration). The user signs in with Microsoft OAuth or the email/password fallback.
147	
148	**Sign-in flows:**
149	- On first launch (or after sign-out), the Helper's tray panel and Settings panel show **Continue with Microsoft** plus an email + password fallback.
150	- Microsoft sign-in opens the user's system browser, redirects back to the Helper's localhost server at `http://127.0.0.1:47380/auth/callback`, exchanges the Supabase PKCE auth code for a session, and stores the access + refresh tokens in the OS keychain via `safeStorage`.
151	- Email/password sign-in calls `ipc:sign-in`, which POSTs to `${supabaseUrl}/auth/v1/token?grant_type=password` with the `apikey` header set to the Supabase anon key.
152	- The Supabase anon key is **not hardcoded**. It is auto-discovered at sign-in time by fetching `${damUrl}/dam-config.json` (field: `supabase_anon_key`) and saved to `userData/config.json` as `supabaseAnonKey`.
153	
154	**OAuth users** can use Microsoft directly in the Helper. The "Helper App Password" path remains a fallback for users who cannot use Microsoft OAuth in the desktop app. It calls `supabase.auth.updateUser({ password })`, which attaches email+password auth to the existing OAuth-linked account — same UUID, same email, OAuth continues to work on the web. No new account is created.
155	
156	### 2. Synology credentials
157	
158	Used as the fallback path for checked-in files when direct SMB/local copy fails. Entered once in Settings under "Synology Credentials". Stored separately from the Supabase session.
159	
160	For USA/Synology mode, the Helper first writes checked-in snapshots directly through the configured local NAS folder mapping (`preferredProvider = "synology"`). Each mapping should point at that PopDAM root's actual mounted folder. For example, if the PopDAM path is `Decor/Character Licensed/foo.ai`, the `Decor` mapping should point at the local `Decor` folder, and the Helper writes `Character Licensed/foo.ai` underneath it. On Windows this can be a UNC path such as `\\edgesynology1\share\Decor`; on macOS the same SMB share must be mounted and exposed as a local path such as `/Volumes/Decor`. The write is temp-copy-then-rename so the final asset path is replaced only after the snapshot copy completes. If that local write fails, the upload queue falls back to Synology File Station using the stored Synology credentials.
161	
162	## Credential storage
163	
164	Both credential sets are encrypted via `safeStorage.encryptString()` (DPAPI on Windows, Keychain on macOS) and stored as base64 blobs in `userData/credentials.enc.json`. No third-party native module is used. See Known Quirks #29.
165	
166	---
167	
168	## Upload queue
169	
170	Failed uploads are persisted to `userData/upload-queue.json` and retried with exponential backoff (up to 5 attempts, `UPLOAD_MAX_RETRIES`). The queue is processed sequentially — one upload at a time. Progress is reported to the renderer via `setProgressCallback`. See Known Quirks #30.
171	
172	---
173	
174	## Build and distribution
175	
176	See `docs/deployment.md` ("POP DAM Helper (Electron)" / "Bridge Agent Deployment" sections) for the full CI/build/distribution workflow.
177	
178	Quick reference:
179	```bash
180	npm run build          # electron-vite production build → out/
181	npm run dist:win       # build + electron-builder --win --x64
182	npm run dist:mac       # build + electron-builder --mac
183	npm run typecheck      # tsc --noEmit on both tsconfigs
184	```
185	
186	---
187	
188	## Auto-update
189	
190	The `publish` block in `electron-builder.yml` points to the GitHub release and `main.ts` calls `autoUpdater.checkForUpdatesAndNotify()` after app startup. The updater path is present, but clean/trusted install and update UX is still limited by code signing:
191	
192	| Platform | Requirement | Cost |
193	|----------|-------------|------|
194	| macOS | Apple Developer account + notarization | $99/yr |
195	| Windows (OV cert) | Auto-update works; SmartScreen warns on every install | ~$60–$150/yr (e.g. Certum) |
196	| Windows (EV cert) | No SmartScreen warning, silent updates | ~$300–$500/yr |
197	
198	Until certificates are configured, users may still see Gatekeeper/SmartScreen warnings and should be prepared to download/reinstall manually from GitHub Releases if auto-update is blocked by OS trust policy. If signing is revived later, set `CSC_LINK` / `CSC_KEY_PASSWORD` and the Apple notarization secrets in GitHub Actions.

file.content:
Used to call the `helper-api` edge function (checkout tracking, heartbeat, device registration). The user signs in with Microsoft OAuth or the email/password fallback.

**Sign-in flows:**
- On first launch (or after sign-out), the Helper's tray panel and Settings panel show **Continue with Microsoft** plus an email + password fallback.
- Microsoft sign-in opens the user's system browser, redirects back to the Helper's localhost server at `http://127.0.0.1:47380/auth/callback`, exchanges the Supabase PKCE auth code for a session, and stores the access + refresh tokens in the OS keychain via `safeStorage`.
- Email/password sign-in calls `ipc:sign-in`, which POSTs to `${supabaseUrl}/auth/v1/token?grant_type=password` with the `apikey` header set to the Supabase anon key.
- The Supabase anon key is **not hardcoded**. It is auto-discovered at sign-in time by fetching `${damUrl}/dam-config.json` (field: `supabase_anon_key`) and saved to `userData/config.json` as `supabaseAnonKey`.

**OAuth users** can use Microsoft directly in the Helper. The "Helper App Password" path remains a fallback for users who cannot use Microsoft OAuth in the desktop app. It calls `supabase.auth.updateUser({ password })`, which attaches email+password auth to the existing OAuth-linked account — same UUID, same email, OAuth continues to work on the web. No new account is created.

### 2. Synology credentials

Used as the fallback path for checked-in files when direct SMB/local copy fails. Entered once in Settings under "Synology Credentials". Stored separately from the Supabase session.

For USA/Synology mode, the Helper first writes checked-in snapshots directly through the configured local NAS folder mapping (`preferredProvider = "synology"`). Each mapping should point at that PopDAM root's actual mounted folder. For example, if the PopDAM path is `Decor/Character Licensed/foo.ai`, the `Decor` mapping should point at the local `Decor` folder, and the Helper writes `Character Licensed/foo.ai` underneath it. On Windows this can be a UNC path such as `\\edgesynology1\share\Decor`; on macOS the same SMB share must be mounted and exposed as a local path such as `/Volumes/Decor`. The write is temp-copy-then-rename so the final asset path is replaced only after the snapshot copy completes. If that local write fails, the upload queue falls back to Synology File Station using the stored Synology credentials.

## Credential storage

Both credential sets are encrypted via `safeStorage.encryptString()` (DPAPI on Windows, Keychain on macOS) and stored as base64 blobs in `userData/credentials.enc.json`. No third-party native module is used. See Known Quirks #29.

---

## Upload queue

Failed uploads are persisted to `userData/upload-queue.json` and retried with exponential backoff (up to 5 attempts, `UPLOAD_MAX_RETRIES`). The queue is processed sequentially — one upload at a time. Progress is reported to the renderer via `setProgressCallback`. See Known Quirks #30.

---

## Build and distribution

See `docs/deployment.md` ("POP DAM Helper (Electron)" / "Bridge Agent Deployment" sections) for the full CI/build/distribution workflow.

Quick reference:
```bash
npm run build          # electron-vite production build → out/
npm run dist:win       # build + electron-builder --win --x64
npm run dist:mac       # build + electron-builder --mac
npm run typecheck      # tsc --noEmit on both tsconfigs
```

---

## Auto-update

The `publish` block in `electron-builder.yml` points to the GitHub release and `main.ts` calls `autoUpdater.checkForUpdatesAndNotify()` after app startup. The updater path is present, but clean/trusted install and update UX is still limited by code signing:

| Platform | Requirement | Cost |
|----------|-------------|------|
| macOS | Apple Developer account + notarization | $99/yr |
| Windows (OV cert) | Auto-update works; SmartScreen warns on every install | ~$60–$150/yr (e.g. Certum) |
| Windows (EV cert) | No SmartScreen warning, silent updates | ~$300–$500/yr |

Until certificates are configured, users may still see Gatekeeper/SmartScreen warnings and should be prepared to download/reinstall manually from GitHub Releases if auto-update is blocked by OS trust policy. If signing is revived later, set `CSC_LINK` / `CSC_KEY_PASSWORD` and the Apple notarization secrets in GitHub Actions.

input.old_string: - Microsoft sign-in opens the user's system browser, redirects back to the Helper's localhost server at `http://127.0.0.1:47380/auth/callback`, exchanges the Supabase PKCE auth code for a session, and stores the access + refresh tokens in the OS keychain via `safeStorage`.
input.new_string:
- Microsoft sign-in opens the user's system browser, redirects back to the Helper's localhost server at `http://127.0.0.1:47380/auth/callback`, exchanges the Supabase PKCE auth code for a session, and stores the access + refresh tokens in the OS keychain via `safeStorage`.
  - ⚠️ **Do not pass a custom `state` to `/auth/v1/authorize`** (`apps/popdam-helper/src/main/oauth.ts`). Supabase GoTrue generates and stores its **own** OAuth state (keyed to a `flow_state` row) and validates it on the provider callback. If the Helper sets its own `state`, GoTrue forwards that string to Microsoft verbatim, then can't find it in its flow store on the callback and fails with **`bad_oauth_state`** ("OAuth state parameter is invalid") — landing the user on the project **Site URL** (`crm.designflow.app`, shared across the apps on this Supabase project) instead of the localhost callback. PKCE (the `code_verifier`) is what binds this flow; no caller `state` is needed. This was the v1.4.1 Microsoft-login bug, fixed in **v1.4.2** (2026-06-25).

The `publish` block in `electron-builder.yml` points to the GitHub release and `main.ts` calls `autoUpdater.checkForUpdatesAndNotify()` after app startup. The updater path is present, but clean/trusted install and update UX is still limited by code signing:

| Platform | Requirement | Cost |
|----------|-------------|------|

| Windows (EV cert) | No SmartScreen warning, silent updates | ~$300–$500/yr |

input.new_string:
The `publish` block in `electron-builder.yml` points to the GitHub release and `main.ts` calls `autoUpdater.checkForUpdatesAndNotify()` after app startup. The updater path uses **`electron-updater`, which only supports the NSIS (Windows) / DMG (macOS) targets** — do **not** switch the Windows target to MSI to solve install/uninstall issues, it would break auto-update.

**Code signing is permanently abandoned — installers stay unsigned forever** (user decision, 2026-06-25). The Apple Developer **Account Holder** cert hurdle (plus a separate Windows OV/EV cert) is too high to justify, so the first-launch Gatekeeper ("right-click → Open") / SmartScreen ("More info → Run anyway") warnings are the **accepted permanent UX**, not pending work. The CI signing wiring (`CSC_LINK`/`CSC_KEY_PASSWORD` + `APPLE_*`) is left dormant only so a future maintainer could revive it. For reference if it ever is (not planned):

| Platform | Requirement | Cost |
|----------|-------------|------|
| macOS | Apple Developer account + notarization | $99/yr |
| Windows (OV cert) | Auto-update works; SmartScreen warns on every install | ~$60–$150/yr (e.g. Certum) |
| Windows (EV cert) | No SmartScreen warning, silent updates | ~$300–$500/yr |

Because installs are unsigned, users may see OS trust warnings and should be prepared to download/reinstall manually from GitHub Releases if auto-update is ever blocked by OS trust policy. See `HANDOFF.md` §5.3.

### Windows uninstall — "NSIS Error: Error launching installer" (Fixed 2026-06-25)

The NSIS uninstaller copies itself to `%TEMP%` and relaunches that copy to delete its own folder; "Error launching installer" means that relaunch failed. A CI bug caused this 100% of the time: the Windows job cached `~\AppData\Local\electron-builder\Cache` — the NSIS **toolchain** that stamps the (un)installer — so a corrupted cache entry shipped a broken uninstaller on every build. Fixed by caching only the immutable Electron binary download, never the toolchain (commit `d7a1133`). **Never cache the electron-builder toolchain dir in CI.** Full writeup + the second possible cause of this error (Windows blocking the unsigned temp copy, which only signing fixes — not relevant here): `docs/KNOWN_QUIRKS.md` #54. To recover an affected machine, just reinstall over the top — the corrected installer writes a fresh, working uninstaller; no manual file deletion needed.

+### Windows uninstall — "NSIS Error: Error launching installer" (Fixed 2026-06-25)

+The NSIS uninstaller copies itself to `%TEMP%` and relaunches that copy to delete its own folder; "Error launching installer" means that relaunch failed. A CI bug caused this 100% of the time: the Windows job cached `~\AppData\Local\electron-builder\Cache` — the NSIS **toolchain** that stamps the (un)installer — so a corrupted cache entry shipped a broken uninstaller on every build. Fixed by caching only the immutable Electron binary download, never the toolchain (commit `d7a1133`). **Never cache the electron-builder toolchain dir in CI.** Full writeup + the second possible cause of this error (Windows blocking the unsigned temp copy, which only signing fixes — not relevant here): `docs/KNOWN_QUIRKS.md` #54. To recover an affected machine, just reinstall over the top — the corrected installer writes a fresh, working uninstaller; no manual file deletion needed.

content.text:
All documentation committed and pushed (`3f27414`). Final report:

## Documentation Updates
| File | Change |
|---|---|
| `docs/KNOWN_QUIRKS.md` | New **#54** — full writeup of the Windows uninstall "NSIS Error: Error launching installer": what the error means (temp-relaunch failure), the root cause (CI cached `electron-builder\Cache` — the NSIS toolchain — so a corrupted entry shipped a broken uninstaller every build), the fix, MSI dead-end, and the never-cache-a-build-toolchain rule. |
| `docs/POPDAM_HELPER.md` | Topic detail: no-custom-`state` OAuth rule (`bad_oauth_state`); signing permanently abandoned; `electron-updater`↔NSIS coupling (don't switch to MSI); Windows uninstall NSIS fix section. |
| `AGENTS.md` | New critical-incident entry (Resolved 2026-06-25); Helper-section CI-caching rule + OAuth `state` rule; signing marked permanently abandoned. |
| `HANDOFF.md` | §5.2 pilot status note (both v1.4.2 blockers fixed); §5.3 + header + Thread B: signing permanently abandoned (was "deferred"). |

Code/CI committed earlier this session: `apps/popdam-helper/src/main/oauth.ts` (OAuth fix, v1.4.2), `.github/workflows/publish-popdam-helper.yml` (toolchain-cache fix).

## Handoff
- `HANDOFF.md`: **present** (correctly)
- Reason: Other workstreams remain open — Brazil/Seafile pilot still to be run end-to-end, PO sync blocked on PLM auth (§5.8), Master Data PLM-canonical matching (§5.11). The Helper login + uninstall fixes are done/verified and noted there; they don't close the file.

## Verification
- Docs verified against session findings: **yes** — every claim traces to inspected code (`oauth.ts`, `electron-builder.yml`, the workflow), the live `authorize`-endpoint probe (GoTrue forwarding custom state), the CI build log (cache restore + 78 MB installer + bundled `elevate.exe`), or the user's confirmed "uninstall works."
- Secrets added: **no**
- Shared-db (`u2giants/shared-db`): **intentionally not updated.** This session made no shared-backend change — only Helper client code, CI, and docs. The Supabase Auth redirect allow-list was modified in a prior session (2026-06-24), not this one. The `bad_oauth_state` finding is specific to the Helper's hand-rolled PKCE flow; the web apps (CRM/PM) use supabase-js `signInWithOAuth`, which never passes a custom `state`, so they won't hit it. The only shared fact (the project Site URL is `crm.designflow.app`) is minor and already implied by existing CRM cutover notes. Note: `shared-db/` here is a read-only auto-synced mirror — it must not be hand-edited anyway.
- Unknowns remaining: The fix confirmed the failure was the corrupted cached toolchain (cause #1). If a *future* freshly-built unsigned uninstaller ever fails again, that would be cause #2 (Windows SmartScreen/Smart App Control blocking the unsigned temp copy), whose only clean fix is code signing — documented in KNOWN_QUIRKS #54 for whoever hits it.

content.text: Now I understand the Helper flow: `checkout()` adds to its in-memory `active` map **only after** the source file is resolved + copied to the workspace (line 244). Any failure before that → "no files checked out" in the Helper, while the server row already exists (web button flips). Now I need to know which server call creates that row and what's actually failing. Let me check the edge function and the live data/logs.

content.text:
The architecture is now clear and it points to a real bug. **Only `/checkouts/start` (called by the Helper) creates the `asset_checkouts` row** — so the web button flipping to "Check In" proves the Helper *did* receive the deep link and successfully created the server-side lock. The failure is **after** that: source resolution / copy / open throws (lines 143–250), so the checkout is never added to the Helper's in-memory `active` map → "no files checked out", and the error is swallowed (`handleDeepLink` just logs it). That also orphans the server lock, which is why your button is stuck on "Check In".

Let me confirm against the live DB (Virginia project) — the orphaned checkout and whether the device registered.

content.text: Now I have the picture. The asset is `Decor/Character Licensed/…/BGX62MVDP01_ART.ai` — squarely inside the configured **Character Licensed** Seafile library. The device is `albt16`, Windows, on v1.4.2. So the Helper resolves `preferred=seafile`, tries SeaDrive, and if that's unavailable falls back to Synology. Let me read the Seafile health/resolve logic to pin down exactly what makes it fail and confirm there's no resolution bug.

lastPrompt: Check-out didn't work. the program installs successfully, logs in successfully. the 'open DAM' button in the helper successfully opens the DAM website. but when i click 'check out' the help still show…

content.text: Resolution logic is correct (the path maps cleanly to the `Character Licensed` library), so this isn't a mapping bug — the Helper genuinely couldn't find the file locally (SeaDrive not mounted/synced, or no Synology mapping) and then **swallowed the error and orphaned the lock**. That silent-failure path is the real defect. Let me see how the tray surfaces state so I can wire a visible error + lock release.

I have what I need. The resolution code is correct — this was a genuine local-availability failure (SeaDrive not mounted/synced, or no Synology mapping) that the Helper **swallowed silently and left the server lock orphaned**. That's the real, fixable defect. I'll make two code fixes: (1) release the server lock when checkout fails after it's created, and (2) surface the error to the user instead of showing a blank "no files checked out."

First, the lock-release in `checkout()`:

content.content: The file /worksp/popdam/apps/popdam-helper/src/main/checkoutManager.ts has been updated successfully. (file state is current in your context — no need to Read it back)

lines: +  // From here on the server has already created the checkout lock. If anything
+  // below throws (source not synced locally, no folder mapping, hydration
+  // timeout, copy error), we must release that lock — otherwise the asset is
+  // left orphaned "checked out" with nothing in the Helper, and the web UI is
+  // stuck on "Check In" forever. Release + rethrow so the caller can surface it.
+    return await resolveCopyAndOpen(checkout_id, asset, root_mappings, config);
+      `Checkout ${checkout_id} failed after the server lock was created — releasing the lock:`,

A checkout that failed AFTER the server created the lock (source file not
synced locally, no folder mapping, hydration timeout, copy error) was a silent
black hole: handleDeepLink swallowed the error, the checkout was never added to
the in-memory map, so the Helper just showed "no files checked out" with zero
feedback — and the server-side lock stayed active forever, stranding the web UI
on "Check In".

  logging it.
- checkoutManager: the Synology-fallback error now carries the actual Seafile
  reason ("SeaDrive client not detected", "Libraries not mounted: …", hydration
  timeout) so a SeaDrive-not-ready state no longer masquerades as a missing
  Synology folder mapping.

content.text:
I traced the actual cause from the live database, not guesswork. Here's exactly what happened and what I fixed.

**What the data showed:** Your checkout of `BGX62MVDP01_ART.ai` (path `Decor/Character Licensed/…`) did reach the Helper — it successfully created the server-side lock (that's why the web button flipped to "Check In"). But the step right after — finding that file locally and copying it — failed, and the Helper **swallowed the error silently** and left the lock orphaned. That's why you saw "no files checked out" with no explanation, and why the button got stuck. Your device `albt16` (Windows, v1.4.2) is registered fine; the file just wasn't available locally.

**What I fixed in code (v1.4.3, building now):**
1. A failed checkout now **releases the server lock automatically** instead of orphaning it — no more stuck "Check In".
2. The Helper now **shows you the real error** (desktop notification) instead of a blank "no files checked out".
3. The error message now names the actual reason (e.g. "SeaDrive client not detected" / "Libraries not mounted" / hydration timeout) instead of a misleading "set up folder mappings."

**I also released your current stuck lock** — the web button will return to "Check Out" within ~15s.

**Why checkout couldn't complete (the part you need to act on):** the Helper can only open a file it can reach on the local disk. For this asset it needs **one** of these set up on `albt16`, and right now neither is:
- **SeaDrive** (the Brazil/work-from-home path): SeaDrive installed + signed in, with the **Character Licensed** library synced so `%USERPROFILE%\SeaDrive\Character Licensed\…` exists.
- **Synology SMB** (the office path): in Helper **Settings → folder mappings**, map the `Decor` root to the mounted NAS folder (e.g. a Windows UNC path like `\\edgesynology1\<share>\Decor`).

One expectation to correct: on success the Helper **copies the file into a private workspace and opens that copy** — it does not just open File Explorer to the SeaDrive folder. So "open the seadrive folder" isn't what it's designed to do.

To give you the exact setup steps rather than both, one question:

questions.question: Which environment is the `albt16` machine testing from? This determines whether checkout should pull files via SeaDrive or the office NAS over SMB.

options.description: You're testing the work-from-home path. I'll give you the SeaDrive install + sign-in + library-sync steps so the Character Licensed library appears under %USERPROFILE%\SeaDrive.

options.label: Not sure / both
options.description: You're not sure which the file should come from. I'll explain how to tell which is reachable from albt16 and pick the simplest working path.

content.text: You're simulating the Brazil/SeaDrive path on a Windows box (`albt16`). One important nuance to verify before I hand you steps: on Windows, the official SeaDrive client mounts as a **drive letter** (e.g. `S:\`), but the Helper's auto-detection defaults to the macOS-style `%USERPROFILE%\SeaDrive`. If there's no way to point the Helper at the drive letter, checkout will fail on Windows even with SeaDrive fully synced. Let me check the Helper's Settings UI and detection.

content.text:
That's the answer. Your Windows SeaDrive mounts at `C:\seadrive\Albert H\My Libraries\` — **not** the `%USERPROFILE%\SeaDrive` the Helper auto-detects — so `detectSeaDriveRoot` returned nothing, Seafile was marked unavailable, and the checkout fell through and failed. You need to point the Helper at that folder.

But reading the health logic, I also found a **second real bug**: the Helper only uses Seafile if **every** configured library is mounted (`health.available` is all-or-nothing). So even after you set the mount folder, a `Character Licensed` checkout would still fail if `Generic Decor` isn't also synced. The per-checkout decision should check the **specific** library the asset needs, not all of them. Let me fix that.

content.content: The file /worksp/popdam/apps/popdam-helper/src/main/checkoutManager.ts has been updated successfully. (file state is current in your context — no need to Read it back)

lines: +    // Gate on the mount root existing — NOT on health.available, which is
+    // all-or-nothing (it requires EVERY configured library to be mounted, so a
+    // Character Licensed checkout would wrongly fail just because Generic Decor
+    // isn't synced). resolveSeafileTarget + ensureHydrated below already verify
+    // the SPECIFIC library and file this asset needs, and fall back if missing.
// Confirm the file is fully local (or trigger + wait for hydration)
+      seafileIssue = health.detail ?? "SeaDrive mount root not found";
log.warn(`Seafile unavailable (${seafileIssue}); falling back to Synology.`);

content.text:
Both the cause and the fixes are now in. Here's where things stand and exactly what to do.

**Root cause (confirmed):** Your SeaDrive mounts at `C:\seadrive\Albert H\My Libraries\` on Windows, but the Helper only auto-detects the Mac-style `%USERPROFILE%\SeaDrive`. So it never found your files, marked Seafile unavailable, fell through, and failed — silently. Two code bugs made it worse (both now fixed): the failure was invisible + orphaned the lock, and the Helper required *all* libraries mounted before using Seafile at all.

**What you need to do (the one manual step I can't do for you):**

1. In the Helper → **Settings → Seafile / SeaDrive → "SeaDrive mount folder"**, enter exactly:
   ```
   C:\seadrive\Albert H\My Libraries
   ```
   (Stop at `My Libraries` — do **not** include `Character Licensed`; the Helper appends the library name itself.) Save.
2. The health panel in that same section should then show **Character Licensed ✓ mounted**.
3. Retry **Check Out** on the asset.

**Use v1.4.4 for this.** Your machine is on 1.4.2, which still has the "all libraries must be synced" bug — so on 1.4.2 the checkout would still fail unless `Generic Decor` is *also* synced. The **v1.4.4** build (~3 min out, run `28195843667`) fixes that so `Character Licensed` works on its own. Re-download from `dam.designflow.app/downloads` once it's up (auto-update may be blocked since we're unsigned).

**One expectation to set:** on success the Helper **copies the file into your private workspace and opens that copy** (Illustrator, for this `.ai`) — it does **not** open File Explorer to the SeaDrive folder. The copy-and-open is the intended design (so your edits are tracked and checked back in).

I also already released your stuck checkout, so the web button is back on "Check Out."

If it still fails after setting the mount folder on v1.4.4, the Helper will now **pop a desktop notification with the exact reason** — send me that text and I'll take it from there. Quick question so I can pre-empt it: under `C:\seadrive\Albert H\My Libraries\`, do you see a `Generic Decor` folder too, or only `Character Licensed`?

  discovered library dir and gives a clear "library isn't visible in SeaDrive
  yet" error if it genuinely can't be found.
- getMountedLibraries/health now reflect discovered locations.

Also closes the last silent-failure path: a check-in upload that exhausts all
retries now marks the checkout 'error' with an actionable message AND fires a
desktop notification, instead of sitting in "uploading" forever (new
setFailedCallback → markUploadFailed, wired in main). Notification logic

content.text:
All pushed as **v1.4.5** (build `28196734748`), and I can confirm the run includes **both** "Build Windows helper" and "Build Mac helper" jobs. Now, your questions:

## 1. Auto-discovery — built it
You're right that the file isn't in the same place on every machine, and non-technical users won't configure paths. v1.4.5 now **discovers the library automatically**. Given any SeaDrive mount, for each library it searches, in order:
- the direct folder, then the standard SeaDrive category folders — **`My Libraries`**, **`Shared with all`**, **`Shared with me`**, and **`Shared with groups\<group>`**;
- and if none of those hit, a **bounded scan** (depth 3, capped at 400 folders so a virtual drive can't hang it).

It also auto-finds the mount itself (your config path, `~/SeaDrive` + per-account subfolders, and Windows `C:\seadrive\<account>`), and **caches** each library's discovered location so later checkouts are instant. **So on v1.4.5 you shouldn't need to set the mount folder manually at all** — your `C:\seadrive\Albert H\My Libraries\Character Licensed` will be found by the scan. If it genuinely can't find a library, you now get a clear "that library isn't visible in SeaDrive yet" message instead of silence.

On your **canary-file idea**: it's a good next step and I deliberately did *not* do it yet because it needs server-side seeding. Name-based search handles every case you listed. The canary would add one thing name-matching can't: disambiguating two libraries that share a folder name, and *confirming* a found folder really is library X by its Seafile ID. If we ever hit a name collision, the plan would be to drop a `.popdam-library-<libraryId>` marker at each library root (via the NAS/Seafile so it syncs down) and match by ID. Flag it if you want me to build that now.

## 2. Mac clients — yes, fully covered
The Helper is a single cross-platform Electron codebase; the workflow builds **both** the Windows `.exe` and the Mac `.dmg` (arm64 + x64) from the same `main` on every push. So **every** fix this session — OAuth, the uninstall NSIS fix, checkout lock-release/error-surfacing, per-library gating, this auto-discovery, and upload-failure surfacing — is in the Mac builds too. The discovery handles the Mac layout (`~/SeaDrive/<account>/My Libraries/…`) as well.

## 3. How check-in works (and where the workspace is)
- **"Private workspace"** = `C:\POP-DAM-Workspace\<checkout-id>\<filename>` on Windows (`~/POP-DAM-Workspace/…` on Mac). On checkout the Helper **copies** the source file there and opens **that copy** — so your edits are isolated and tracked.
- **Check-in does *not* copy back into the SeaDrive folder.** It snapshots your workspace file and **uploads it to the Synology NAS** — for SeaDrive-sourced checkouts via the Synology **File Station API** (reachable from Brazil over Tailscale); for office/Synology mode via direct SMB. The **NAS is the source of truth, and Seafile mirrors the NAS**, so your change syncs *back down* into SeaDrive afterward. For SeaDrive check-ins the lock is held in "verifying" until the bridge agent confirms the file landed intact on the NAS.
- **Implication you'll hit:** check-in needs **Synology credentials** set in Helper Settings and NAS reachability (Tailscale for Brazil). Without them, the upload fails — and now (see below) it tells you so instead of hanging.

## 4. "Are ALL failures verbose now?"
Honest answer: **the failure paths that strand you or hide an action you need to take are now all verbose**, but not literally every internal hiccup pops a dialog (by design). Specifically:

Now surfaced (desktop notification + error state):
- Checkout failure → **releases the server lock** + notification with the real reason.
- Check-in failure → notification.
- **Permanent upload failure** (exhausted retries) → checkout flips to **error** with an actionable message + notification (this was the last silent one — previously it sat in "uploading" forever; fixed this push).

Intentionally quiet (logged only, not popped): heartbeat failures, the optional Seafile `obj_id` lookup, startup checkout-list load, config-save errors. These self-heal or degrade gracefully and don't require user action — popping a toast on every failed 30-second heartbeat would be noise. **Everything** lands in the log file regardless: `%APPDATA%\popdam-helper\logs\main.log` (Windows) / `~/Library/Logs/popdam-helper/main.log` (Mac).

---

**Next step for you:** once v1.4.5 is up (~3 min, re-download from `dam.designflow.app/downloads`), just try **Check Out** — no manual mount-folder setup needed. If it opens the file from your workspace, checkout works; then set Synology credentials in Settings before testing check-in. If anything fails, you'll now get a notification with the exact reason — send me that text.

Want me to document the auto-discovery + check-in data-flow in `docs/POPDAM_HELPER.md` while it's fresh?

content.text:
v1.4.6 is building. Straight answers to all four:

## 1. Does closing the file check it back in? Does the Helper even know it was closed?
**No, and largely no.** Check-in is **explicit** — you click "Check In" in the tray (or the web button). Closing Illustrator does nothing automatically. The Helper watches your workspace copy for **edits** (saves) and **deletion** via a filesystem watcher, but it **cannot reliably know an app *closed* a file** — "closed" is an application event, not a filesystem one; detecting it would need fragile, per-app file-handle monitoring. So auto-check-in-on-close isn't something I'd trust to be correct.

This is exactly the gap behind your #3 concern, so I addressed it from the other direction (quit guard + reminders — see #3). If you'd prefer, I can add **auto-check-in when the file has been idle/unchanged for N minutes** — but that risks uploading half-finished edits, which is why the design is explicit. Your call.

## 2. Does it ask for the Synology credentials and put them in the right place?
**It tells you, but doesn't auto-prompt.** On a credential-less check-in it now says "Synology credentials not configured — open Settings" (modal). You enter the Synology username/password once in **Settings → Synology Credentials**, and they're stored encrypted in the OS keychain (Windows DPAPI / macOS Keychain) — the right place. It does **not** pre-fill or pop an inline login. I can make that failure **auto-open Settings to the credentials field** so it's one click — want that?

## 3. No silent failures — "4 hours and never knew it didn't save" (fixed, v1.4.6)
This is the one I treated as a directive. Now:
- **A check-in upload that permanently fails** → a **modal dialog** ("Check-in failed — file NOT saved to the server"), the popup opens, a desktop notification fires, **and** the checkout stays as a red "error" card with the reason. Impossible to miss, and persistent until you retry.
- **Quitting the Helper with files still checked out / mid-upload** → a **warning dialog requiring confirmation** ("Files not yet saved to the server… they will not finish uploading until you reopen and check in"). This catches "close and walk away."
- Everything is also in the log: `%APPDATA%\popdam-helper\logs\main.log` / `~/Library/Logs/popdam-helper/main.log`.

**One honest remaining gap:** if someone edits for hours and simply **never clicks Check In** (and leaves the Helper running), nothing nags them — the file shows as "Checked out" but the Helper has no way to know they consider it "done." The strongest fix is a **periodic reminder** for files that have been edited but not checked in for, say, 30–60 min. I didn't add nagging unilaterally, but I think you want it — say the word and I'll ship it.

## 4. Why didn't it tell you the SeaDrive folder wasn't linked? (fixed)
Because that **was** the silent-failure bug — before v1.4.3 the checkout swallowed the "SeaDrive not found" error entirely. It's fixed three ways now:
- **v1.4.3:** any failed checkout pops a notification with the real reason instead of silently showing "no files checked out."
- **v1.4.5:** a clear message — "the *Character Licensed* library isn't visible in SeaDrive yet" — instead of a misleading mappings error.
- **v1.4.5 also auto-discovers** the library, so in your case it won't even fail — no manual linking needed.

The Settings panel also shows per-library mounted/missing status. If you want it to **proactively** warn at startup ("⚠ Character Licensed not found in SeaDrive") rather than only at checkout time, I can add that too.

---

So: **#3 and #4 are fixed and shipping in v1.4.6.** For #1, #2, and the "never-checked-in reminder" in #3, those are behavior choices — tell me which you want and I'll build them. My recommendation: **yes to the periodic un-checked-in reminder** (directly kills the "4 hours" risk) and **yes to auto-opening Settings on the credentials error** (removes a stumble for the Brazil designers). Want both?

content.text:
**#1, #3, #4 are done and shipping in v1.4.7** (build `28199350193`):

- **#1 — credential failure auto-opens Settings:** a check-in that fails for missing Synology credentials now fails *fast* (no 5-retry wait) and opens the Helper straight to the **Synology Credentials** field, scrolled into view and highlighted.
- **#3 — 60-min reminder:** any file checked out and editable for >60 min that isn't on the server yet fires a "don't forget to check in" notification, re-nagging hourly. This is the direct countermeasure to the "worked for hours, never saved" trap.
- **#4 — proactive startup warning:** on the SeaDrive/WFH path, if SeaDrive isn't found at all, or none of your PopDAM libraries are visible, you get notified at launch — not only when a checkout fails.

## #2 — Adobe close-detection plugins: the honest assessment

I did **not** ship this one, because doing it blind would be irresponsible — let me explain the real constraints so you can decide:

**Photoshop: feasible.** PS has a proper event API (UXP `addNotificationListener(["close"])`, or a CEP extension). A small plugin can detect a document close and ping the Helper's localhost server. Solid.

**Illustrator: this is the hard blocker.** Illustrator does **not** expose a document-close event to plugins — its scripting/event model is far weaker than Photoshop's. The only workaround is a CEP panel that *polls* the open-document list and infers "it disappeared = closed." That's hacky, requires the panel to be running, and isn't event-driven. Your test file was a `.ai`, so the case you most need is the weakest one.

**Packaging into the installer has real cost:** CEP extensions must be **signed** (a cert — which we just decided is too costly) *or* the installer has to flip Adobe's **PlayerDebugMode** registry/plist flag to allow unsigned extensions, then copy files into Adobe's per-version extension folders. That's invasive, version-fragile, and I **cannot test any of it** from here (no Adobe apps in this environment). Bundling untested extension code + Adobe-config edits into everyone's installer is exactly the kind of thing that breaks installs silently.

**The robust alternative I'd recommend instead:** detect file close at the **OS level inside the Helper** — watch whether any process still holds the workspace file open (`lsof` on macOS, Restart Manager on Windows). This is **app-agnostic** (works for Photoshop, Illustrator, and anything else), needs **no Adobe plugin, no signing, no registry hacks**, and I *can* build and test it. It pairs with the protections I just shipped.

So my recommendation: let me build the **OS file-handle "file closed" detection** (reliable, both apps, testable) rather than the Adobe plugins. If you still specifically want an in-app "Check in now?" button inside Photoshop/Illustrator, I'd scope the **Photoshop UXP plugin** as its own tested deliverable and treat Illustrator as best-effort polling.

How do you want to proceed on #2?
- **A)** OS-level file-close detection in the Helper (my pick — reliable, both apps), or
- **B)** Adobe plugins (PS solid, AI hacky/polling, needs PlayerDebugMode + can't be tested here), or
- **C)** both?

The other three are already in v1.4.7 — re-download once it's green (~3 min) to test.

content: Goal: get the PopDAM Helper checkout/check-in working for the Brazil/SeaDrive pilot. Shipped v1.4.7 with the credentials, reminder, and library-warning fixes. Next: you decide how to handle #2, Adobe close-detection — OS-level file detection, Adobe plugins, or both.
OS file-handle "file closed" detection is fine for me. but will it truly detect the file being closed if the application is still open?

content.text:
Sharp question — and the honest answer is **probably not, and you've caught a real flaw in that plan.**

## Why file-handle detection won't reliably work for Adobe
Photoshop and Illustrator (like most desktop apps) **don't hold the file open while you edit.** The typical pattern is:
1. Open the file → read the whole thing into memory → **release the OS handle immediately**.
2. You edit in memory for hours — **no handle is held, the file on disk isn't locked**.
3. Save → briefly open the handle → write → release again.

So if the Helper polled "is any process holding this file open?", the answer would be **"no" for ~99% of the time the document is actually open** in Illustrator. It would constantly read as "closed" right after you opened it. Handle-detection only catches the split-seconds during open and save — it genuinely cannot distinguish "open but idle in memory" from "closed." Trying an exclusive-open lock check has the same problem (the file isn't locked while open).

Bottom line: **file-handle detection is the wrong tool for "did the user close the document."** I'd have been selling you something that doesn't deliver, so I'm flagging it now rather than shipping it.

## What the OS *can* reliably tell us — and it's actually better
The Helper **already detects every save** — its file watcher fires whenever the workspace copy is written. That's a *stronger* signal than "closed" for your actual goal (nothing silently lost), because:
- The moment you save in Illustrator, the Helper knows there are **local edits not yet on the server**.
- It doesn't matter whether you keep the document open for 5 minutes or 5 days — we can track "edited since checkout, not checked in" reliably, no plugin required.

So instead of chasing "closed," I'd build **edit-aware safety**: once the watcher sees a save, mark the checkout "edited – not checked in" (distinct badge), and let the hourly reminder + quit guard key off *that* ("you've edited this and it's not on the server") rather than mere age. That directly kills the "worked 4 hours, never saved" risk and is fully testable.

## The only thing that truly knows open/closed
…is the app itself — i.e. the **Adobe plugin** route. Photoshop can fire a real close event; Illustrator can only be polled (watch its open-document list shrink). Same caveats as before (PlayerDebugMode for unsigned, can't test here, AI is hacky). It's the *only* way to get a genuine "user closed the document" prompt, if that specific UX is what you want.

## My recommendation
Drop file-handle close-detection (it won't work), and instead let me build **edit-aware reminders**: the watcher already sees saves, so I'll make the Helper distinguish "checked out & edited but not checked in" and drive the reminders/quit-guard/badge off that. Reliable, no Adobe dependency, no signing.

One related thing I noticed while checking the watcher: if Illustrator saves via atomic rename-replace (write temp → rename over the original), the current watcher could misread that as the file being *deleted*. If we go with (A), I'll harden the watcher to handle rename-replace saves correctly at the same time.

content.text: Now a README with install steps, and a convenience "reveal plugin folder" action. Let me write the README and check the IPC file to add a reveal handler:

input.content:
# POP DAM Helper — Photoshop plugin

Detects when you **close** a checked-out file in Photoshop and tells the POP DAM
Helper, which then offers to check the file back in. Photoshop-only (Illustrator
has no document-close event for plugins).

## How it works
- The plugin keeps a live map of open document IDs → file paths.
- On a document `close` event it POSTs `{ event: "documentClosed", path }` to the
  running Helper at `http://127.0.0.1:47380/editor-event`.
- The Helper matches that path to an active checkout that has un-checked-in edits
  and pops "Check it in now?".

## Install (pilot / development)
The plugin is **unsigned**, so it can't be distributed through Creative Cloud.
Load it with Adobe's **UXP Developer Tool (UDT)**:

1. Install **UXP Developer Tool** from the Creative Cloud app (free).
2. Open UDT → **Add Plugin** → select this folder's `manifest.json`.
3. Select the plugin row → **Load**. (Photoshop must be running.)
4. In Photoshop: **Plugins → POP DAM Helper** to show the panel. The panel
   shows "Connected to POP DAM Helper" when the Helper is running.

`manifest.json` lives inside the installed Helper at:
- **Windows:** `…\resources\photoshop-plugin\manifest.json`
- **macOS:** `POP DAM Helper.app/Contents/Resources/photoshop-plugin/manifest.json`

The Helper's Settings has a **"Reveal Photoshop plugin folder"** button that opens
this location for you.

## Notes / limitations
- Requires Photoshop 23.0+ (UXP).
- A fully silent auto-install isn't possible for an unsigned UXP plugin; UDT
  sideload is the supported path for the pilot.

content.text:
Both parts shipped in **v1.4.8** (build `28216031619`).

## Part A — edit-aware reminders (reliable, no editor dependency)
- **Watcher hardened for atomic saves:** the Helper now watches the checkout's workspace *directory* (not the single file), so Illustrator/Photoshop's write-temp-then-rename saves register as **edits** instead of being misread as "file deleted." A genuine delete is re-confirmed after a delay before flagging an error.
- **Edit tracking:** a checkout is marked `editedSinceCheckout` the moment its file is saved (and inferred from file mtime after a Helper restart).
- **Reminders now fire on edits, not mere age:** the hourly nudge only triggers for files that are **edited but not checked in** — no noise for untouched checkouts — and is keyed off the last edit time ("edited 2h ago, not checked in").
- **Quit guard + tray badge** now distinguish **"Edited — not checked in"** so the at-risk files are obvious.

This is the robust answer to "worked 4 hours, never saved": the Helper knows the moment you save locally that there are edits not on the server, regardless of whether you ever close the app.

## Part B — Photoshop close-detection plugin (Illustrator dropped)
- **Helper side (done + verified by typecheck):** new `POST /editor-event` endpoint on the localhost server; when Photoshop reports closing a checked-out file that has un-checked-in edits, the Helper pops **"You closed an edited file — check it in now?"** and runs the check-in if you say yes.
- **The plugin (UXP):** ships inside the installer (`resources/photoshop-plugin/`). It listens for Photoshop's document-close event, maps document→path, and POSTs to the Helper. Settings has a **"Reveal Photoshop plugin folder"** button.

**Honest caveats on the plugin (as I flagged):**
- I **can't end-to-end test it here** — there's no Photoshop in this environment. The Helper-side endpoint and wiring are verified; the plugin's PS event API (`addNotificationListener(["close"])`, document path mapping) needs a real Photoshop to confirm. Expect a small amount of on-device tuning.
- **Install is via Adobe's UXP Developer Tool** (point it at the bundled `manifest.json`). Because we're unsigned, it can't go through Creative Cloud, and there's no fully-silent auto-install — UDT sideload is the pilot path. Steps are in the plugin's README and the Settings button reveals the folder.

So: the **edit-aware safety net (Part A) is the reliable, always-on protection**, and the **Photoshop plugin is a bonus** that adds a close-time prompt once you sideload it. Re-download v1.4.8 when it's green to test Part A immediately; for the plugin, load it via UDT and close an edited checked-out PSD to confirm the prompt fires (tell me what happens and I'll tune it).

Want me to capture all of this Helper behavior (edit-tracking, reminders, quit guard, the `/editor-event` contract, and the plugin install) in `docs/POPDAM_HELPER.md` so it's not just in commit messages?

file.content:
## Localhost HTTP server (port 47380)

The Helper starts an HTTP server bound to `127.0.0.1:47380` on launch. This lets the web DAM's directory browser bypass the cloud bridge agent for local filesystem listings.

**Endpoints:**

| Method | Path | Response |
|--------|------|----------|
| `GET` | `/status` | `{ ok, version, roots[] }` |
| `GET` | `/browse?path=X` | `{ ok, path, entries: DirEntry[], listed_at }` |

**Path resolution for `/browse`:** `path=""` returns configured root mappings. `path="root_id/sub/dir"` resolves the first segment as a `root_id` against `config.rootMappings`, joins the remainder as a subpath. If the first segment is not a known root ID, the full path is treated as an absolute local path (power-user mode).

**CORS:** Restricted to `*.designflow.app` and `localhost`. Other origins get a 403.

**Port:** Fixed at 47380 (hardcoded in `localServer.ts` and probed in `DirectoryBrowserTab.tsx`). See Known Quirks #32 for why it's fixed rather than dynamic.

**Failure mode:** `EADDRINUSE` logs a warning and the Helper continues — the directory browser falls back to the bridge agent path automatically.

---

## Web DAM integration (`DirectoryBrowserTab.tsx`)

On mount, the component probes `http://localhost:47380/status` with a 2-second timeout:
- **Helper running:** all directory listings go directly to `GET /browse?path=...` — instant response.
- **Helper not running:** existing Supabase Realtime + bridge agent path is used unchanged.
- A green "Local helper" badge appears in the card header when the fast path is active.
- If the Helper crashes mid-session, the next browse call fails, `helperAvailable` is set to `false`, and the component retries via the bridge agent.

---

## Checkout state machine

```
active

---

## Upload queue

Failed uploads are persisted to `userData/upload-queue.json` and retried with exponential backoff (up to 5 attempts, `UPLOAD_MAX_RETRIES`). The queue is processed sequentially — one upload at a time. Progress is reported to the renderer via `setProgressCallback`. See Known Quirks #30.

---

## Build and distribution

---
input.new_string:
## Upload queue

Failed uploads are persisted to `userData/upload-queue.json` and retried with exponential backoff (up to 5 attempts, `UPLOAD_MAX_RETRIES`). The queue is processed sequentially — one upload at a time. Progress is reported to the renderer via `setProgressCallback`. See Known Quirks #30.

**Missing Synology credentials fail fast.** A `/credentials? not configured/i` error is not retried 5× — it's marked failed immediately and routed through the failure callback so the user is prompted at once (main opens Settings to the credentials field; see "No silent failures").

---

## SeaDrive library auto-discovery (`seafileAdapter.ts`)

SeaDrive does **not** mount a library in one fixed place. The mount root varies by OS and by the account name chosen at sign-in (Windows: `C:\seadrive\<account>\`, macOS: `~/SeaDrive/<account>/`), and within a mount a library sits under whichever category folder it's shared through: `My Libraries\<lib>`, `Shared with all\<lib>`, `Shared with me\<lib>`, or `Shared with groups\<group>\<lib>`. Non-technical users won't configure exact paths, and the location differs per machine — so the Helper **discovers** it:

- `seaDriveBaseRoots(config)` collects every plausible base mount (the configured `seaDriveRoot`, the platform default + its per-account subfolders, Windows `C:\seadrive\<account>`).
- `findLibraryDir(seaDriveFolder, config)` searches each base: the direct folder, then the known category folders, then `Shared with groups/<group>/`, then a **bounded** breadth-first fallback (depth 3, 400-node cap so a virtual drive can't hang the scan).
- The hit is cached per library in `config.seafileLibraryPaths` (keyed by the library's `seaDriveFolder` name) and re-validated on use, so later checkouts are instant.
- `resolveSeafileTarget` resolves via the discovered library dir (not a hard-coded `<root>/<library>`); if a library genuinely can't be found it throws a clear "library isn't visible in SeaDrive yet" error.

**Why checkout gates on `health.root`, not `health.available`:** `getSeafileHealth().available` is all-or-nothing (true only if *every* configured library is found). `checkoutManager` gates on the mount existing and lets `resolveSeafileTarget` verify the **specific** library the asset needs — otherwise a `Character Licensed` checkout would wrongly fail just because `Generic Decor` isn't synced. (See Known Quirks; the all-or-nothing gate was a bug, fixed 2026-06-25.)

---

## Edit tracking, reminders & quit guard

The risk this addresses: a designer edits a checked-out file for hours and never realizes it isn't on the server (check-in is **explicit** — closing the editor does nothing on its own).

- **Edit detection.** The file watcher (`checkoutManager.watchFile`) watches the checkout's workspace **directory** (depth 0, `atomic: true`), *not* the single file, so editors that save via atomic rename-replace (Illustrator/Photoshop write a temp file then rename over the original) register as a normal **edit** rather than being misread as "file deleted." A real `unlink` is re-confirmed after a 2.5 s delay before the checkout is flagged `error`.
- A save sets `editedSinceCheckout` + `lastEditedAt` on the in-memory `CheckoutRecord`. On restart these are inferred from the workspace file's mtime vs the checkout time.
- **Hourly reminder** (`main.ts` `remindStaleCheckouts`, checked every 10 min): fires a desktop notification only for `active` checkouts that are **edited but not checked in** and whose last edit is >60 min old (re-nags hourly). Untouched checkouts are never nagged — nothing to lose.
- **Quit guard** (`before-quit`): if files are still checked out, quitting is intercepted with a confirm dialog; the wording escalates when any file is edited/uploading/verifying/error ("edits that are NOT on the server yet").
- The tray popup shows an **"Edited — not checked in"** badge distinct from "Checked out".

---

## No silent failures

Failure paths that could otherwise strand a user are surfaced; only self-healing best-effort operations stay quiet (logged). Centralized in `tray.showNotification`.

- **Checkout failure** (`protocol.handleDeepLink`): releases the just-created server lock (so the asset isn't orphaned "checked out") **and** shows a notification with the real reason — never a silent "no files checked out".
- **Permanent upload failure** (`uploadQueue` → `setFailedCallback`): marks the checkout `error`, opens the popup, fires a notification, **and** pops a modal dialog ("Check-in failed — file NOT saved to the server"). For missing Synology credentials it instead opens Settings to the credentials field (highlighted).
- **Proactive startup check** (`warnIfSeaDriveLibrariesMissing`): on the Seafile/WFH path, if SeaDrive isn't found at all — or none of the configured libraries are visible — the user is notified at launch, not only when a checkout fails. (Only warns when *nothing* is found; a user may legitimately have access to just some libraries — Settings shows the per-library breakdown.)
- **Intentionally quiet** (logged only): heartbeat, the optional Seafile `obj_id` lookup, startup checkout-list load, config-save errors. All failures are in the log regardless: `%APPDATA%\popdam-helper\logs\main.log` (Windows) / `~/Library/Logs/popdam-helper/main.log` (macOS).

---

## Photoshop integration (`resources/photoshop-plugin/`)

Optional. Lets Photoshop tell the Helper when a checked-out file is **closed**, so the Helper can offer to check it in immediately. **Photoshop-only** — Illustrator exposes no document-close event to plugins, so it is intentionally not supported.

- **Plugin** (UXP, `resources/photoshop-plugin/`, ships in the installer via `extraResources`): keeps a live map of open `documentID → file path` (refreshed on open/select/save), listens for the `close` event, and POSTs `{ event: "documentClosed", path }` to the Helper at `http://127.0.0.1:47380/editor-event`. Requires PS 23.0+ and declares `network` permission for `http://127.0.0.1:47380`.
- **Helper side:** `localServer` accepts `POST /editor-event` → `setEditorEventCallback`. `main` looks up the active checkout by workspace path (`findCheckoutByWorkspacePath`); if it's `active` **and** edited, it pops a "Check it in now?" dialog and runs `checkin()` on confirm. A `ping` event (empty path) is a no-op used by the panel to show connection status.
- **Install (pilot):** the plugin is **unsigned**, so it can't go through Creative Cloud — load it with Adobe's **UXP Developer Tool** pointed at the bundled `manifest.json`. Settings → "Reveal Photoshop plugin folder" opens the location; full steps in `resources/photoshop-plugin/README.md`. A fully silent auto-install isn't possible for an unsigned UXP plugin.
- **Status:** Helper-side endpoint + wiring are verified by typecheck; the plugin's PS event behavior needs on-device testing (no Photoshop in CI). Introduced Helper v1.4.8.

---

+- **Plugin** (UXP, `resources/photoshop-plugin/`, ships in the installer via `extraResources`): keeps a live map of open `documentID → file path` (refreshed on open/select/save), listens for the `close` event, and POSTs `{ event: "documentClosed", path }` to the Helper at `http://127.0.0.1:47380/editor-event`. Requires PS 23.0+ and declares `network` permission for `http://127.0.0.1:47380`.

+- **Helper side:** `localServer` accepts `POST /editor-event` → `setEditorEventCallback`. `main` looks up the active checkout by workspace path (`findCheckoutByWorkspacePath`); if it's `active` **and** edited, it pops a "Check it in now?" dialog and runs `checkin()` on confirm. A `ping` event (empty path) is a no-op used by the panel to show connection status.

+- **Install (pilot):** the plugin is **unsigned**, so it can't go through Creative Cloud — load it with Adobe's **UXP Developer Tool** pointed at the bundled `manifest.json`. Settings → "Reveal Photoshop plugin folder" opens the location; full steps in `resources/photoshop-plugin/README.md`. A fully silent auto-install isn't possible for an unsigned UXP plugin.
+- **Status:** Helper-side endpoint + wiring are verified by typecheck; the plugin's PS event behavior needs on-device testing (no Photoshop in CI). Introduced Helper v1.4.8.
See `docs/deployment.md` ("POP DAM Helper (Electron)" / "Bridge Agent Deployment" sections) for the full CI/build/distribution workflow.

Documented and pushed (`60dff02`). This is a docs-only commit, so it didn't trigger a Helper build.

What's now in `docs/POPDAM_HELPER.md`:
- **SeaDrive library auto-discovery** — how `seaDriveBaseRoots` / `findLibraryDir` search the category folders with a bounded scan + per-library cache, and *why* checkout gates on `health.root` not the all-or-nothing `health.available`.
- **Edit tracking, reminders & quit guard** — the atomic-save-aware directory watcher, `editedSinceCheckout`/`lastEditedAt`, the edits-only hourly reminder, the quit guard, and the "Edited — not checked in" badge.
- **No silent failures** — exactly which paths surface (checkout lock-release + notify, modal on upload failure, creds→Settings, proactive startup library warning) and which stay logged-only, plus the log file locations.
- **Photoshop integration** — the UXP plugin, the `POST /editor-event` contract, the Helper-side dialog flow, the unsigned/UDT install reality, and the Illustrator-has-no-close-event limitation.
- Updated the endpoints table (`/auth/callback`, `POST /editor-event`).
- A one-line pointer in `AGENTS.md` so future sessions find it.

That wraps up everything from this thread: the checkout/check-in bugs are fixed and documented, edit-aware safety + the Photoshop plugin are shipped in **v1.4.8**, and the docs reflect it all. - The transient untracked `supabase/migrations/20260626002000_fix_core_customer_leftovers.sql` in shared-db (appeared, then gone within seconds — its own branch, now pushed to origin).

# AI Session Documentation Update Prompt

Use this at the end of an AI coding session when the session changed code, deployment behavior, data flow, configuration, operational assumptions, or important project knowledge.

Your job is not to rebuild the entire documentation system. 

-----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Update only the Markdown files that need durable knowledge from this specific session.

## Goal

Record what this session learned or changed so future developers and future AI sessions do not have to rediscover it.

Focus on:
- Information from this session that will still matter later.
- Idiosyncrasies that may look wrong but are intentional.
- Mistakes, dead ends, or assumptions from this session that future sessions should avoid.
- Architecture, data flow, deployment, configuration, or workflow changes.
- New operational steps, rollback notes, feature flags, identifiers, limits, or dependencies.
- Any temporary/incomplete work that needs a handoff.

## Rules

- Derive every doc update from code, config, migrations, workflows, commands run, or verified session findings.
- Do not document guesses as facts. Mark unknowns clearly and say how to verify them.
- Do not add secrets, tokens, passwords, private keys, or credential values.
- Do not duplicate large explanations across multiple docs. Put the short routing/context note in `AGENTS.md` only if future AI sessions need to see it early; put topic detail in the relevant doc under `docs/`.
- If the change does not require documentation updates, say so explicitly in the final report.

## What To Update

- `AGENTS.md`: only for high-signal guidance future AI sessions must see quickly, including new quirks, critical warnings, task routing, identifiers, or "do not repeat this mistake" notes.
- `HANDOFF.md`: create or update only if work is unfinished, blocked, partially deployed, or requires continuation context. Delete it only when the unfinished work it describes is truly complete.
- Topic docs under `docs/`: update the specific doc for the affected area, such as architecture, deployment, configuration, schema, worker logic, PopSG, Helper, Seafile, auth, bulk jobs, or known quirks.
- `README.md`: update only if the quick-start or top-level orientation changed.
- `CLAUDE.md`: update only for Claude Code-specific workflow rules. General AI/developer guidance belongs in `AGENTS.md`.

## Shared supabase.com Database / Backend Changes

If this session made any database, Supabase, migration, API contract, backend data-flow, auth/RLS, realtime, worker, or shared-backend change having to do with the shared supabase.com backend, also update the canonical shared database repo:

```text

u2giants/shared-db

https://github.com/u2giants/shared-db

```

This applies even when the code change was made inside an app repo. App repos may contain generated types, frontend adapters, workers, or local notes, but durable backend truth belongs in `shared-db`.

At minimum, document in `u2giants/shared-db`:

- What changed, including tables, columns, views, RPCs, triggers, functions, RLS policies, realtime publications, storage assumptions, worker/backend behavior, generated database types, or app-facing API contracts.

- Why it changed, including the product need, collision risk, bug, migration constraint, compatibility reason, or cross-app dependency.

- Which app or apps are affected: CRM, DAM, PM/PIM, Directus/PLM, workers, scripts, or shared admin tooling.

- Where the durable implementation lives: migration filenames, SQL files, scripts, API views/RPC names, worker files, generated type files, and relevant app repo commits if known.

- What was verified: local checks, `scripts/check-sql.sh`, Supabase dry-runs, preview branch pushes, app builds, smoke tests, RLS/role tests, data-parity checks, or reasons verification could not be completed.

- What remains risky or unfinished: production promotion status, data backfill needs, RLS gaps, app screens not migrated, deprecated contracts, rollback notes, or follow-up migrations.

Use the existing `shared-db` documentation structure:

- Put cross-app operating rules or urgent warnings in `AGENTS.md` only when future AI sessions must see them immediately.

- Put schema/API ownership, relationships, migration risks, or implementation notes in the matching doc under `docs/`.

- Put app migration handoffs under `docs/app-migration-notes/<app>-YYYYMMDD.md`.

- Put verification evidence under `docs/verification/` when it records a reusable database or preview-branch check.

- Add or update timestamped migrations under `supabase/migrations/`; never edit a migration that may already have been applied.

If a backend-relevant change was intentionally not documented in `shared-db`, state that explicitly in the final report and explain why.

## Required Session Note Shape

When adding session-derived knowledge, prefer concise entries like:

```md
### [Short decision or warning name]

What changed:
[One or two sentences.]

Why:
[Constraint, incident, bug, user decision, or operational reason.]

Future sessions should:
[Concrete instruction: what to do, what to avoid, where to verify.]
```

For unfinished work, include:

```md
## [Workstream name]

Status:
[done / partial / blocked / not started]

Done:
[Verified completed items.]

Next action:
[Exact next step.]

Risks / watchouts:
[Anything future sessions must not miss.]
```

## Verification Before Final Response

- Docs changed are limited to files that actually needed updates.
- New claims match the repository or verified session findings.
- No secrets were added.
- `HANDOFF.md` exists only if continuation context is needed.
- Any new quirk or warning explains why it exists and what would break if ignored.

## Final Report

Reply with:

```md
## Documentation Updates
| File | Change |
|---|---|
| `path/to/file.md` | Short summary |

## Handoff
- `HANDOFF.md`: present/absent
- Reason: ...

## Verification
- Docs verified against session findings: yes/no
- Secrets added: yes/no
- Unknowns remaining: ...
```


Before you finish, make sure no repo has untracked files, especially /worksp/shared-db. If you create a shared-db migration, put it on its own branch or clean it up.
Before you end, leave the repo in a handoff-safe state.

If work is complete:
- run the required checks
- commit/merge/push/deploy according to the repo instructions
- confirm the working tree is clean, or list exactly which untracked/modified files remain and why

If work is not complete:
- do not leave mystery untracked files
- create or update HANDOFF.md
- list every changed/untracked file, what it is for, whether it is safe to keep, and the next exact action
- do not say “done” if anything still needs commit/merge/apply
Because shared-db affects multiple apps, do not leave untracked migrations or docs without either finishing the branch/PR/merge workflow or writing HANDOFF.md explaining exactly what remains.

push and commit

content.content:
78	### 5.2 Brazil/Seafile pilot (after 5.1)
79	**Helper v1.4.2 (2026-06-25) fixed two pilot blockers found while testing:** (a) "Continue with Microsoft" returned `bad_oauth_state` — the Helper was passing its own `state` to `/auth/v1/authorize`; removed it so GoTrue manages state (PKCE binds the flow). (b) Windows uninstall failed 100% with "NSIS Error: Error launching installer" — CI was caching the NSIS toolchain and shipped a corrupted uninstaller; fixed by not caching it (`docs/KNOWN_QUIRKS.md` #54). Both verified working. Supabase Auth allows the Helper redirect URL `http://127.0.0.1:47380/auth/callback` on live project `qsllyeztdwjgirsysgai` (verified 2026-06-24 via Supabase Management API). On one Brazil Mac: (1) install official **SeaDrive** (`dam.designflow.app/downloads` → SeaDrive card, or seafile.com) and sign in with the designer's Microsoft account; (2) confirm the `Character Licensed` + `Generic Decor` libraries appear under `~/SeaDrive` and sync; (3) install the **Helper** (`dam.designflow.app/downloads`), sign in with **Continue with Microsoft**, and in Helper **Settings → Seafile/SeaDrive** confirm "Preferred source for checkout" is **Seafile** and confirm the mount root; (4) in PopDAM web, **Check Out & Open** a Decor asset and confirm the Helper resolves it from `~/SeaDrive`, hydrates, and opens it; check it back in. Provider auto-selection by region is **not built** (5.4); the default is Seafile, and office/USA users manually switch to Synology. Success = checkout/check-in round-trips a real file via Seafile, with the Synology/Tailscale fallback covering any not-yet-synced file.
80	
81	### 5.3 Helper code signing (ABANDONED — installers stay unsigned permanently)
82	Status: **will not be done.** As confirmed by the user on 2026-06-25, the Helper installers (macOS + Windows) **will always remain unsigned** — the certificate/identity hurdle (Apple Developer **Account Holder** role for the Developer ID cert; a separate OV/EV cert for Windows) is too high to justify. Do **not** treat this as pending work, and do not reopen it in handoffs. The unsigned first-launch warnings (macOS Gatekeeper right-click→Open; Windows SmartScreen "More info → Run anyway") are the accepted, permanent UX. The CI wiring below is left in place only so a future maintainer *could* revive it if the calculus ever changes — not because anyone intends to.

### 5.2 Brazil/Seafile pilot (after 5.1)
**Helper v1.4.2 (2026-06-25) fixed two pilot blockers found while testing:** (a) "Continue with Microsoft" returned `bad_oauth_state` — the Helper was passing its own `state` to `/auth/v1/authorize`; removed it so GoTrue manages state (PKCE binds the flow). (b) Windows uninstall failed 100% with "NSIS Error: Error launching installer" — CI was caching the NSIS toolchain and shipped a corrupted uninstaller; fixed by not caching it (`docs/KNOWN_QUIRKS.md` #54). Both verified working. Supabase Auth allows the Helper redirect URL `http://127.0.0.1:47380/auth/callback` on live project `qsllyeztdwjgirsysgai` (verified 2026-06-24 via Supabase Management API). On one Brazil Mac: (1) install official **SeaDrive** (`dam.designflow.app/downloads` → SeaDrive card, or seafile.com) and sign in with the designer's Microsoft account; (2) confirm the `Character Licensed` + `Generic Decor` libraries appear under `~/SeaDrive` and sync; (3) install the **Helper** (`dam.designflow.app/downloads`), sign in with **Continue with Microsoft**, and in Helper **Settings → Seafile/SeaDrive** confirm "Preferred source for checkout" is **Seafile** and confirm the mount root; (4) in PopDAM web, **Check Out & Open** a Decor asset and confirm the Helper resolves it from `~/SeaDrive`, hydrates, and opens it; check it back in. Provider auto-selection by region is **not built** (5.4); the default is Seafile, and office/USA users manually switch to Synology. Success = checkout/check-in round-trips a real file via Seafile, with the Synology/Tailscale fallback covering any not-yet-synced file.

### 5.3 Helper code signing (ABANDONED — installers stay unsigned permanently)
Status: **will not be done.** As confirmed by the user on 2026-06-25, the Helper installers (macOS + Windows) **will always remain unsigned** — the certificate/identity hurdle (Apple Developer **Account Holder** role for the Developer ID cert; a separate OV/EV cert for Windows) is too high to justify. Do **not** treat this as pending work, and do not reopen it in handoffs. The unsigned first-launch warnings (macOS Gatekeeper right-click→Open; Windows SmartScreen "More info → Run anyway") are the accepted, permanent UX. The CI wiring below is left in place only so a future maintainer *could* revive it if the calculus ever changes — not because anyone intends to.
